EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-moving, somewhat moisture starved upper level storm system moved across the Northern Plains February 7-8, producing 2 to 4 inches of snow across portions of far southeast South Dakota into adjacent portions of southwest Minnesota. While winds were not particularly strong, occasional wind gusts from 20 to 30 mph produced some patchy blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 2.9 inches in Flandreau.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Brief high winds followed a strong cold front during the afternoon of February 24 across Beadle County, resulting in a couple locations reporting wind gusts in excess of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong negatively tilted trough lifted northeast through the Central Plains and resulted in the rapid intensification of low pressure as it moved from Kansas to northern Illinois. Conditions started on the warmer side with mainly light rain, drizzle, and a few thunderstorms on the morning of March 4, but a quick transition to snow occurred by evening and was accompanied by a drastic increase in winds to 45 to 60 mph. While snowfall ended up generally less than an inch across South Dakota, lack of existing snow cover prior to this system limited the areas for which the strong winds resulted in blizzard conditions. Much of southwest Minnesota, northwest Iowa, and far northeast Nebraska saw closed or impassible roads by the early morning of March 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds were sustained at times from 40 to 44 mph with gusts as high as 58 mph near Corsica.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level disturbance from Canada moved southeast across the Dakotas into Minnesota February 10-11, resulting in periods of light to moderate snowfall. The highest snow totals, exceeding 6 inches in some places, fell along a narrow west to east band just south of Interstate 90 across southeast South Dakota, coincident with the strongest low level frontogenesis. An arctic air mass followed the snowfall, resulting in many locations reporting wind chill values of 25 below or colder the morning of February 11.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 7.5 inches 7.2 miles north of Parker and 6.0 inches at Bridgewater.

Winds would be strong enough to damage several small trees on the south side of the city of Aberdeen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level disturbance from Canada moved southeast across the Dakotas into Minnesota February 10-11, resulting in periods of light to moderate snowfall. The highest snow totals, exceeding 6 inches in some places, fell along a narrow west to east band just south of Interstate 90 across southeast South Dakota, coincident with the strongest low level frontogenesis. An arctic air mass followed the snowfall, resulting in many locations reporting wind chill values of 25 below or colder the morning of February 11.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures ranged from 25 to 34 below zero during the early morning hours. A South Dakota State University Mesonet site 7 miles west-southwest of Beresford briefly dipped as low as 38 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system across western Kansas lifted northeastward into Iowa March 29-30. Given a very moist airmass and ample lift, precipitation rapidly blossomed across South Dakota and southern Minnesota during the afternoon of March 29. Precipitation initially fell as rain, but quickly transitioned to snow for areas west of the James River Valley as temperatures dropped below freezing. Two to five inches of heavy, wet snow accumulated in these areas through the late evening. Farther east, a mix of snow and ice accumulated across parts of Brookings County into the early morning hours of March 30.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 5.5 inches in Platte and 3.0 inches 2 miles northeast of Academy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two episodes of snowfall occurred across south central South Dakota as a strong mid-level wave dropped from the central Rockies into the Southern Plains. The initial snowfall on January 4 was associated with mid-level warm advection and frontogenesis, which was limited in eastward extent by dry air to the east. The second period of snow during the afternoon into the early overnight of January 5 was a result of moisture wrapping back into the inverted trough which extended into south central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall over the period totaled 4.0 inches 9 miles south of Dallas and 2.5 inches at Gregory.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong negatively tilted trough lifted northeast through the Central Plains and resulted in the rapid intensification of low pressure as it moved from Kansas to northern Illinois. Conditions started on the warmer side with mainly light rain, drizzle, and a few thunderstorms on the morning of March 4, but a quick transition to snow occurred by evening and was accompanied by a drastic increase in winds to 45 to 60 mph. While snowfall ended up generally less than an inch across South Dakota, lack of existing snow cover prior to this system limited the areas for which the strong winds resulted in blizzard conditions. Much of southwest Minnesota, northwest Iowa, and far northeast Nebraska saw closed or impassible roads by the early morning of March 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds were sustained from 40 to 45 mph at times, with gusts as high as 60 mph at Wakonda.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.5 inches 3.4 miles east-southeast of Sioux Falls, 3.3 inches 5.2 miles east of Sioux Falls, 3.1 inches at the Sioux Falls Regional Airport (KFSD), and 2.4 inches 0.5 miles north of Hartford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two distinct supercell clusters developed across central South Dakota during the late afternoon of July 28 within a highly unstable environment (MUCAPE up to 5000 J/kg) ahead of an eastward advancing surface front. The southern supercell cluster produced three tornadoes across Gregory County, the strongest of which was rated EF2. ||Scattered severe wind gusts were reported across southeast South Dakota as the thunderstorms moved east before the two clusters merged near and south of Sioux Falls. Aided by increasing deep layer shear from the north, a linear structure rapidly developed with a derecho racing eastward into northwest Iowa. Winds gusted from 80 to 100 mph along the derecho's path with widespread tree and structural damage across the area. In addition, three embedded short-lived tornadoes were documented within the line of thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of damaging thunderstorm wind gusts was measured by a Road Weather Information System sensor near Parker. The initial severe gusts were reached at 1946 CST with a peak of 85 mph at 1953 CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving from Nebraska into Minnesota generated strong winds across central, north central and northeast South Dakota. A peak wind gust of 68 mph was recorded at a mesonet station 2 miles north of the Pierre Regional Airport, with a 63 mph gust at the South Dakota Department of Transportation weather station 3 miles southeast of Reliance.

A strong Colorado Low ejected into the Central and Northern Plains March 14-15. On the warm side of the system and to the north of the low track, thunderstorms rotated northwestward across northwest Iowa, northeast Nebraska, and southeast South Dakota during the late afternoon and early evening of March 14. The strongest storms produced isolated pockets of severe wind gusts ranging from 58 to 63 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 62 mph thunderstorm wind gust was measured by the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site near Gayville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two distinct supercell clusters developed across central South Dakota during the late afternoon of July 28 within a highly unstable environment (MUCAPE up to 5000 J/kg) ahead of an eastward advancing surface front. The southern supercell cluster produced three tornadoes across Gregory County, the strongest of which was rated EF2. ||Scattered severe wind gusts were reported across southeast South Dakota as the thunderstorms moved east before the two clusters merged near and south of Sioux Falls. Aided by increasing deep layer shear from the north, a linear structure rapidly developed with a derecho racing eastward into northwest Iowa. Winds gusted from 80 to 100 mph along the derecho's path with widespread tree and structural damage across the area. In addition, three embedded short-lived tornadoes were documented within the line of thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts were measured by a personal weather station.

Low pressure moving from Nebraska into Minnesota generated strong winds across central, north central and northeast South Dakota. A peak wind gust of 68 mph was recorded at a mesonet station 2 miles north of the Pierre Regional Airport, with a 63 mph gust at the South Dakota Department of Transportation weather station 3 miles southeast of Reliance.

Continued mild and dry conditions in March would result in an expansion of drought and high fire danger conditions. Thankfully during this period of observed fires, winds remained relatively light aiding in containment.  A fire on March 9th by Enemy Swim housing complex burned about 55 acres and also damaged powerlines. An additional fire was observed at that time on satellite in Buffalo county, with local emergency management responding that it was a fire that was consuming approximately 400-500 hay bales.  On March 10th, a fire southwest of Aberdeen would also go on to burn about 135 acres in addition to engulfing approximately 500 hay bales.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire consumed approximately 500 hay bales before running through vegetation for nearly a mile, stopping short of a neighbors farmhouse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A system to the south initially brought a wintry mix to northeast and south central South Dakota, before transitioning to a band of heavier snowfall over the Watertown area. The Watertown COOP observer would record 8.1 inches of snow through the course of the day. In addition to the ice and snow, wind gusts topped out at 58 mph at the South Dakota Department of transportation (SDDOT) weather stations at Brandt, with a peak gust of 53 mph at the SDDOT sites at Macs Corner, Wallace and Doland. Widespread winds in excess of 45 mph and falling snow resulted in hazardous travel and several slide-ins.  A semi jackknifed blocking portions of Interstate 90 west of Chamberlain and another semi jackknifed blocking portions of Interstate 29 north of Watertown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two semitrucks were observed in the ditch, while another had jackknifed in the westbound lane of Interstate 90.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air surged over the Northern Plains and remained across the area for several days. The coldest air and lowest wind chill values in our area were across far northwest South Dakota, where air temperatures were as low as around 35 below, with wind chill values as low as 45 to 50 below during the overnight and early morning hours for several days. Elsewhere across the plains from northwest into south central South Dakota, wind chill values dropped to 30 to 40 below at times during the overnight and early morning for 1 to 2 days.

A cold front and upper-level disturbance passed across the area, bringing snowfall to much of northwest South Dakota during the daytime and early evening hours. Snowfall amounts across far northwest South Dakota were mostly in the 2 to 4 inch range, with amounts to near 6 inches in the Lemmon area.

A low pressure system moved from the Colorado Rockies during the evening of March 6 and across the Central Plains through mid-day on March 7. A band of precipitation developed along strong mid-level frontogenesis to the north of the low track. With temperatures initially above freezing, precipitation fell as rain, but quickly transitioned to snow and accumulated a broad swath of 2 to 4 inches mainly along and south of Interstate 90. Higher totals from 5 to 7 inches fell across south-central South Dakota. Winds were relatively light and the wet snow minimized overall reductions in visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.0 inches 7.5 miles south-southwest of Chamberlain, 2.5 inches 5 miles south of Chamberlain, and 2.0 inches 1.9 miles south of Pukwana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of Arctic high pressure made a slow progression across the Northern and Central Plains starting on January 17th and finally departing late on January 21st. Wind chill values on the morning of January 19th generally ranged between 30 to 40 degrees below zero, with a wind chill of 47 degrees below zero observed by the South Dakota Department of Transportation weather station near Summit. There was little improvement on January 20th, with temperatures starting in the teens to low 20s below zero and wind chills well below 40 degrees below zero, with the coldest observed wind chill of 54 degrees below zero at the Remote Automated Weather Station 13 miles northeast of Eureka. 12 other weather stations would record a minimum wind chill of 50 degrees below zero or colder.  With the high pressure overhead, temperatures failed to warm above the zero degree mark across all of central, north central and northeast South Dakota. Temperatures again fell into the teens and low 20s below zero for the morning of January 21st, with many stations recording minimum wind chills in the 30 to 40 degrees below zero range.  Again the coldest wind chill reading was recorded by the Department of Transportation weather station near Summit at 47 degrees below zero.

An arctic cold front passed through the region, bringing very cold temperatures from the 18th to the morning of the 21st. The coldest temperatures and lowest wind chills were during the overnight and morning hours of the 20th. Temperatures dropped to 10 below to 30 below zero in many areas, with northerly winds of 15 to over 30 mph producing wind chill values as low as 30 below to around 50 below zero.

Drought conditions persist, with limited improvement, across portions of north-central and south-central South Dakota through the month of January. In north-central South Dakota, the South Dakota State University (SDSU) Mesonet stations near Lantry, Parade, and La Plant recorded only about a quarter-inch of moisture or less. The McLaughlin COOP observer reported just 0.16 inches of moisture for the month. In south-central South Dakota, the Murdo COOP observed 0.57 inches of moisture equivalent from 8.6 inches of snow. However, this was a standout observation, as the SDSU Mesonet station 12 miles south of Vivian recorded only 0.18 inches of moisture, while the station 6 miles south-southeast of Kennebec reported just 0.16 inches.

A passing cold front brought gusty northwest winds and areas of light snow to much of western South Dakota. Periods of heavier upslope enhanced snowfall developed over the northern Black Hills on the 22nd. Snowfall totals of 4 to 8 inches were common from Lead and Deadwood to Cheyenne Crossing and O'Neill Pass. Some areas across the northern Foothills received 2 to 4 inches. The gusty winds produced blowing and drifting snow, along with some travel impacts across the area.

An upper-level trough and cold front swept through the region, bringing some light snowfall and gusty winds to much of the area. A period of heavy upslope snowfall occurred during the daytime and evening hours on the 11th across the higher elevations of the northern Black Hills. Snowfall totals there were 8 to 16 inches, with 2 to around 6 inches across the lower elevations. Snowfall totals of 1 to 4 inches were noted over much of the northern and northeastern foothills of the Black Hills. Gusty winds to over 40 mph on the 11th produced some blowing and drifting snow. The heavy snowfall over the higher elevations caused travel impacts.

High winds followed behind a cold front that moved through during the daytime hours Jan 17th.  A peak wind gust of 62 mph was recorded at the Department of Transportation weather station near Summit. The Department of Transportation also reported multiple semi-trucks that were blown off Interstate 29. The peak wind gusts at the Sisseton ASOS nearby was 58 mph. Numerous additional weather stations gusted in the 50 to 55 mph range behind the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple semi-trucks and trucks with trailers were blown off Interstate 29. The peak wind gusts at the Sisseton ASOS nearby was 58 mph. The highest wind gust in Roberts county was observed at the DOT station near Summit with a peak gust of 62 mph.

There was little improvement on January 20th, with temperatures starting in the teens to low 20s below zero and wind chills well below 40 degrees below zero, with the coldest observed wind chill of 54 degrees below zero at the Remote Automated Weather Station 13 miles northeast of Eureka. 12 other weather stations would record a minimum wind chill of 50 degrees below zero or colder.  With the high pressure overhead, temperatures failed to warm above the zero degree mark across all of central, north central and northeast South Dakota. Temperatures again fell into the teens and low 20s below zero for the morning of January 21st, with many stations recording minimum wind chills in the 30 to 40 degrees below zero range.  Again the coldest wind chill reading was recorded by the Department of Transportation weather station near Summit at 47 degrees below zero.

A cold front passed through the area during the morning and midday hours, bringing a period of very gusty west to northwest winds to a good portion of the northwest and west central South Dakota plains. Some wind gusts to around 60 mph were noted across the area during the late morning and afternoon hours.

A low pressure system moved from the Colorado Rockies during the evening of March 6 and across the Central Plains through mid-day on March 7. A band of precipitation developed along strong mid-level frontogenesis to the north of the low track. With temperatures initially above freezing, precipitation fell as rain, but quickly transitioned to snow and accumulated a broad swath of 2 to 4 inches mainly along and south of Interstate 90. Higher totals from 5 to 7 inches fell across south-central South Dakota. Winds were relatively light and the wet snow minimized overall reductions in visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 5.0 inches 7.6 miles west-northwest of Springfield and 3.5 inches at Tyndall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of snow set up across north central South Dakota. 9.5 inches was recorded by the public in Herreid, with 8 inches observed by COOP observers in Pollock and 1 mile southeast of Artas. COOP observers in Bowdle and 2 miles southeast of McLaughlin also recorded totals at 5 inches, with several other reports of 2 to 4 inches scattered across north central and northeast South Dakota.

A cold front followed by high pressure generated strong winds across north central and central South Dakota, but the core of strongest winds passed mainly through northeast South Dakota during the morning of Friday, February 28th. A peak wind gust of 66 mph was recorded by the South Dakota Department of Transportation (SDDOT) station in Peever, with 65 mph measured by the SDDOT station 7 miles northeast of Milbank. Five additional SDDOT weather stations in the northeast of the state would record a peak wind gust in excess of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weak disturbances interacted with a tight baroclinic zone in place behind an arctic cold front, producing a prolonged period of light snow across much of southwest South Dakota. Snowfall totals were mostly in the 3 to 7 inch range across these areas over about a 36 to 48 hour period. The snow resulted in mostly minor travel impacts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought (D2) conditions continue across north central and south central South Dakota, with an eastward expansion early in February due to antecedent dry conditions. Only 5 to 50 percent of normal precipitation was observed across central and northeast South Dakota during the month. The Pierre Automated Surface Observing System (ASOS)  recorded just 0.05 inches of moisture, nearly 0.7 inches below the February average, while the Faulkton COOP station recorded only 0.04 inches for the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air surged over the Northern Plains and remained across the area for several days. The coldest air and lowest wind chill values in our area were across far northwest South Dakota, where air temperatures were as low as around 35 below, with wind chill values as low as 45 to 50 below during the overnight and early morning hours for several days. Elsewhere across the plains from northwest into south central South Dakota, wind chill values dropped to 30 to 40 below at times during the overnight and early morning for 1 to 2 days.

A fast-moving low pressure system brought thunderstorms in the relatively warmer air ahead of it, along with strong non-thunderstorm winds in the colder air behind it. The strong non-thunderstorm winds developed during the morning across central and north central South Dakota and peaked in the afternoon hours of February 24th. Several South Dakota Department of Transportation weather stations recorded gusts in excess of 60 mph, including a 64 mph wind gusts 13 miles north-northwest of Sansarc, and 62 mph recorded winds 4 miles west-northwest of Cresbard, 1 mile north of Ree Heights, and 3 miles southeast of Reliance. Numerous other locations reported wind gusts between 50 and 60 mph.||Later that afternoon and evening, unusual February thunderstorms developed over eastern South Dakota. While the environment wasn���t favorable for strong storms, there was enough low-level rotation in the atmosphere to support a brief, weak tornado in Codington County. Some storms also produced pea-sized hail. This event was particularly notable, as it marked the first documented tornado in South Dakota history during the month of February.

A band of heavier snowfall passed across far northwest South Dakota as an upper-level disturbance moved across Montana, North Dakota, and far northern South Dakota. Snowfall totals of 2 to 5 inches were reported across much of Harding and Perkins counties. Gusty northwest winds caused patchy blowing snow and reduced visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure made a slow progression through the Northern and Central Plains from February 10th through the 14th, resulting in extreme cold and dangerous wind chills. On the morning of the 11th, low temperatures at Aberdeen and Mobridge reached 26 and 27 degrees below zero, respectively, with widespread wind chill values ranging from 35 to 55 degrees below zero. The coldest observed wind chill that morning was 54 degrees below zero, recorded by the South Dakota State University (SDSU) mesonet station near Trail City. The lowest wind chill observed by the Automated Surface Observation Station (ASOS) at Aberdeen Regional Airport was 44 degrees below zero, while the ASOS at Mobridge Municipal Airport recorded 38 degrees below zero. Daytime temperatures remained frigid, with high temperatures in the single digits on either side of zero. The National Weather Service COOP observer in Pollock reported a high of 9 degrees below zero, while the SDSU mesonet stations in McIntosh and 2 miles north-northwest of Herreid recorded highs of 5 degrees below zero.||On the morning of February 12th, temperatures dropped into the teens to more than 30 degrees below zero. The coldest reading, 34 degrees below zero, was recorded by the SDSU mesonet site 2 miles north-northwest of Herreid. Wind chills at that location fell to 48 degrees below zero, with most other locations experiencing wind chills as low as 30 to 40 degrees below zero. Daytime highs again remained in the single digits, with the SDSU mesonet stations in McIntosh and 2 miles southeast of McLaughlin only warming to 4 degrees below zero.||Overnight into the morning of February 13th, temperatures once again fell into the 20s and 30s below zero. The coldest reading, 36 degrees below zero, was recorded 4 miles north-northwest of Wakpala. The SDSU mesonet stations 4 miles southwest of Mound City and 2 miles southeast of McLaughlin each recorded 31 degrees below zero. The ASOS at Aberdeen Regional Airport reported a low of 28 degrees below zero. Wind chills were similarly severe, with a low of 53 degrees below zero observed at the Herreid mesonet site, and a minimum wind chill of 44 degrees below zero recorded by the Aberdeen ASOS.

A second, more intense surge of Arctic air moved into the Northern and Central Plains between February 16 and 19. Low temperatures on the morning of February 17 ranged from the 20s to 30s below zero. The coldest temperatures were recorded by the South Dakota State University (SDSU) mesonet station located 2 miles southeast of McLaughlin and by a personal Davis weather station 4 miles north-northwest of Wakpala. Thirty-eight additional weather stations recorded low temperatures of 25 degrees below zero or colder. This included the Automated Surface Observing System (ASOS) at Mobridge Regional Airport, which recorded a new record low of 27 degrees below zero. These extremely low temperatures, combined with gusty winds, led to wind chill values ranging from 45 to 50 degrees below zero. The lowest wind chills of 52 degrees below zero were observed near Eureka, McLaughlin, and Artas.||Daytime temperatures remained well below normal. The entire forecast area stayed below zero throughout the day. The coldest high temperature was 17 degrees below zero, observed at the SDSU mesonet near McIntosh. Many locations recorded high temperatures colder than 10 degrees below zero. The ASOS at Aberdeen Regional Airport reached only 6 degrees below zero, and the ASOS at Mobridge Municipal Airport reached 10 degrees below zero, which also set a record for the coldest high temperature on that date.||On the morning of February 18, temperatures dropped to 30 degrees below zero or colder across north-central South Dakota. A personal Davis weather station 4 miles north-northwest of Wakpala recorded the coldest reading at 39 degrees below zero. The Mobridge Municipal Airport ASOS fell to 30 degrees below zero, while the ASOS at Aberdeen Regional Airport dropped to 25 degrees below zero. Wind chill readings were also extremely cold, with 54 degrees below zero recorded at the South Dakota Department of Transportation (SDDOT) weather station near Herreid, and 52 degrees below zero recorded at the SDSU mesonet stations 4 miles southwest of Mound City and 2 miles southeast of McLaughlin. By the afternoon, most locations remained below zero, although a few spots in central South Dakota briefly rose above the zero-degree mark. The lowest high temperatures, each reaching 11 degrees below zero, were observed at the SDSU mesonet station near McIntosh, the SDDOT station near Herreid, and the SDDOT station near Trail City. The Mobridge Municipal Airport ASOS reported a high of just 5 degrees below zero.||Temperatures dropped again overnight into the morning of February 19. The lowest reading of 37 degrees below zero was observed at both the personal Davis weather station 2 miles northwest of Shambo Ranch in far western Corson County and the SDSU mesonet station 4 miles southwest of Mound City. Thirty-five additional weather stations recorded temperatures of 25 degrees below zero or colder. Wind chills remained extremely hazardous, with a low of 55 degrees below zero recorded at the SDSU mesonet 2 miles southeast of McLaughlin. The Mobridge ASOS reported a wind chill of 47 degrees below zero, and the Aberdeen ASOS recorded 41 degrees below zero.||Although winds became lighter, which prevented wind chills from falling below the 45-degree threshold, extreme cold persisted through the evening of February 19 into the morning of February 20. Lows included 31 degrees below zero recorded 4 miles north-northwest of Wakpala and 29 degrees below zero at both the SDSU mesonet stations 4 miles southwest of Mound City and 2 miles southeast of McLaughlin. On the morning of February 20, 29 additional weather stations reported low temperatures of 20 degrees below zero or colder across north-central and northeast South Dakota.

A fast-moving low pressure system brought thunderstorms in the relatively warmer air ahead of it, along with strong non-thunderstorm winds in the colder air behind it. The strong non-thunderstorm winds developed during the morning across central and north central South Dakota and peaked in the afternoon hours of February 24th. Several South Dakota Department of Transportation weather stations recorded gusts in excess of 60 mph, including a 64 mph wind gusts 13 miles north-northwest of Sansarc, and 62 mph recorded winds 4 miles west-northwest of Cresbard, 1 mile north of Ree Heights, and 3 miles southeast of Reliance. Numerous other locations reported wind gusts between 50 and 60 mph.||Later that afternoon and evening, unusual February thunderstorms developed over eastern South Dakota. While the environment wasn���t favorable for strong storms, there was enough low-level rotation in the atmosphere to support a brief, weak tornado in Codington County. Some storms also produced pea-sized hail. This event was particularly notable, as it marked the first documented tornado in South Dakota history during the month of February.

A low pressure system moved from the Colorado Rockies during the evening of March 6 and across the Central Plains through mid-day on March 7. A band of precipitation developed along strong mid-level frontogenesis to the north of the low track. With temperatures initially above freezing, precipitation fell as rain, but quickly transitioned to snow and accumulated a broad swath of 2 to 4 inches mainly along and south of Interstate 90. Higher totals from 5 to 7 inches fell across south-central South Dakota. Winds were relatively light and the wet snow minimized overall reductions in visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.8 inches 0.2 miles south of Hurley, 2.8 inches at Marion, and 2.0 inches 7.2 miles north of Parker.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second, more intense surge of Arctic air moved into the Northern and Central Plains between February 16 and 19. Low temperatures on the morning of February 17 ranged from the 20s to 30s below zero. The coldest temperatures were recorded by the South Dakota State University (SDSU) mesonet station located 2 miles southeast of McLaughlin and by a personal Davis weather station 4 miles north-northwest of Wakpala. Thirty-eight additional weather stations recorded low temperatures of 25 degrees below zero or colder. This included the Automated Surface Observing System (ASOS) at Mobridge Regional Airport, which recorded a new record low of 27 degrees below zero. These extremely low temperatures, combined with gusty winds, led to wind chill values ranging from 45 to 50 degrees below zero. The lowest wind chills of 52 degrees below zero were observed near Eureka, McLaughlin, and Artas.||Daytime temperatures remained well below normal. The entire forecast area stayed below zero throughout the day. The coldest high temperature was 17 degrees below zero, observed at the SDSU mesonet near McIntosh. Many locations recorded high temperatures colder than 10 degrees below zero. The ASOS at Aberdeen Regional Airport reached only 6 degrees below zero, and the ASOS at Mobridge Municipal Airport reached 10 degrees below zero, which also set a record for the coldest high temperature on that date.||On the morning of February 18, temperatures dropped to 30 degrees below zero or colder across north-central South Dakota. A personal Davis weather station 4 miles north-northwest of Wakpala recorded the coldest reading at 39 degrees below zero. The Mobridge Municipal Airport ASOS fell to 30 degrees below zero, while the ASOS at Aberdeen Regional Airport dropped to 25 degrees below zero. Wind chill readings were also extremely cold, with 54 degrees below zero recorded at the South Dakota Department of Transportation (SDDOT) weather station near Herreid, and 52 degrees below zero recorded at the SDSU mesonet stations 4 miles southwest of Mound City and 2 miles southeast of McLaughlin. By the afternoon, most locations remained below zero, although a few spots in central South Dakota briefly rose above the zero-degree mark. The lowest high temperatures, each reaching 11 degrees below zero, were observed at the SDSU mesonet station near McIntosh, the SDDOT station near Herreid, and the SDDOT station near Trail City. The Mobridge Municipal Airport ASOS reported a high of just 5 degrees below zero.||Temperatures dropped again overnight into the morning of February 19. The lowest reading of 37 degrees below zero was observed at both the personal Davis weather station 2 miles northwest of Shambo Ranch in far western Corson County and the SDSU mesonet station 4 miles southwest of Mound City. Thirty-five additional weather stations recorded temperatures of 25 degrees below zero or colder. Wind chills remained extremely hazardous, with a low of 55 degrees below zero recorded at the SDSU mesonet 2 miles southeast of McLaughlin. The Mobridge ASOS reported a wind chill of 47 degrees below zero, and the Aberdeen ASOS recorded 41 degrees below zero.||Although winds became lighter, which prevented wind chills from falling below the 45-degree threshold, extreme cold persisted through the evening of February 19 into the morning of February 20. Lows included 31 degrees below zero recorded 4 miles north-northwest of Wakpala and 29 degrees below zero at both the SDSU mesonet stations 4 miles southwest of Mound City and 2 miles southeast of McLaughlin. On the morning of February 20, 29 additional weather stations reported low temperatures of 20 degrees below zero or colder across north-central and northeast South Dakota.

A fast-moving low pressure system brought thunderstorms in the relatively warmer air ahead of it, along with strong non-thunderstorm winds in the colder air behind it. The strong non-thunderstorm winds developed during the morning across central and north central South Dakota and peaked in the afternoon hours of February 24th. Several South Dakota Department of Transportation weather stations recorded gusts in excess of 60 mph, including a 64 mph wind gusts 13 miles north-northwest of Sansarc, and 62 mph recorded winds 4 miles west-northwest of Cresbard, 1 mile north of Ree Heights, and 3 miles southeast of Reliance. Numerous other locations reported wind gusts between 50 and 60 mph.||Later that afternoon and evening, unusual February thunderstorms developed over eastern South Dakota. While the environment wasn���t favorable for strong storms, there was enough low-level rotation in the atmosphere to support a brief, weak tornado in Codington County. Some storms also produced pea-sized hail. This event was particularly notable, as it marked the first documented tornado in South Dakota history during the month of February.

A second, more intense surge of Arctic air moved into the Northern and Central Plains between February 16 and 19. Low temperatures on the morning of February 17 ranged from the 20s to 30s below zero. The coldest temperatures were recorded by the South Dakota State University (SDSU) mesonet station located 2 miles southeast of McLaughlin and by a personal Davis weather station 4 miles north-northwest of Wakpala. Thirty-eight additional weather stations recorded low temperatures of 25 degrees below zero or colder. This included the Automated Surface Observing System (ASOS) at Mobridge Regional Airport, which recorded a new record low of 27 degrees below zero. These extremely low temperatures, combined with gusty winds, led to wind chill values ranging from 45 to 50 degrees below zero. The lowest wind chills of 52 degrees below zero were observed near Eureka, McLaughlin, and Artas.||Daytime temperatures remained well below normal. The entire forecast area stayed below zero throughout the day. The coldest high temperature was 17 degrees below zero, observed at the SDSU mesonet near McIntosh. Many locations recorded high temperatures colder than 10 degrees below zero. Wind chill readings were also extremely cold, with 54 degrees below zero recorded at the South Dakota Department of Transportation (SDDOT) weather station near Herreid, and 52 degrees below zero recorded at the SDSU mesonet stations 4 miles southwest of Mound City and 2 miles southeast of McLaughlin. By the afternoon, most locations remained below zero, although a few spots in central South Dakota briefly rose above the zero-degree mark. A fast-moving low pressure system brought thunderstorms in the relatively warmer air ahead of it, along with strong non-thunderstorm winds in the colder air behind it. The strong non-thunderstorm winds developed during the morning across central and north central South Dakota and peaked in the afternoon hours of February 24th. Several South Dakota Department of Transportation weather stations recorded gusts in excess of 60 mph, including a 64 mph wind gusts 13 miles north-northwest of Sansarc, and 62 mph recorded winds 4 miles west-northwest of Cresbard, 1 mile north of Ree Heights, and 3 miles southeast of Reliance. Numerous other locations reported wind gusts between 50 and 60 mph.||Later that afternoon and evening, unusual February thunderstorms developed over eastern South Dakota. While the environment wasn���t favorable for strong storms, there was enough low-level rotation in the atmosphere to support a brief, weak tornado in Codington County. Some storms also produced pea-sized hail. This event was particularly notable, as it marked the first documented tornado in South Dakota history during the month of February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved from the Colorado Rockies during the evening of March 6 and across the Central Plains through mid-day on March 7. A band of precipitation developed along strong mid-level frontogenesis to the north of the low track. With temperatures initially above freezing, precipitation fell as rain, but quickly transitioned to snow and accumulated a broad swath of 2 to 4 inches mainly along and south of Interstate 90. Higher totals from 5 to 7 inches fell across south-central South Dakota. Winds were relatively light and the wet snow minimized overall reductions in visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 2 to 4 inches across the Sioux Falls metro area, 2.5 inches at Colton, and 2.0 inches 0.5 miles north of Hartford and 4.1 miles northwest of Renner.

Some wind gusts to around 60 mph were noted across the area during the late morning and afternoon hours.

Mobridge would observe only 0.21 inches of moisture, a half inch shy of normal, while Aberdeen would only receive 0.28 inches, which is 0.61 inches below normal. The mean temperature for the month of March in Mobridge and Aberdeen was 6.6 and 5.7 degrees above normal respectively.

A weak system looping up across Iowa connected with some colder air coming down out of Canada to generate a mix of rain, snow and ice. A band of locally heavy snowfall developed across central South Dakota, with a peak measurement of 6.5 inches recorded by the COOP observer in Pierre and 6.2 inches recorded by the Murdo COOP observer. Meanwhile, the public and spotters submitted ice accumulation measurements of 0.5 inches 11 miles south of Vayland and 0.25 inches 2 miles southwest of Waverly. These conditions made for hazardous travel.  A significant accident along Interstate 90 east of Draper in Jones County involved a bus flipping which resulted in multiple injuries that stressed local hospital resources and caused a temporary closure of Interstate 90.

Departing high pressure created a stiff south breeze, with mild temperatures and low humidity. Drought conditions continued well into the month, and the combination of hot dry weather and dry fuels resulted in fires across north central and northeast South Dakota. The largest of these fires was approximately 1285 acres, 3 miles west of Sunset Colony in Marshall county, while a 290 acre fire ignited near La Plant in Dewey county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire developed in the afternoon 3 miles west of Sunset Colony at a nearby farmstead. The fire spread rapidly into a low marshy area with a large pond, burning mostly into an area designated as Conservation Reserve Program (CRP) land. The Automated Weather Observing System (AWOS) 2 miles north of Britton would record gusts of 35 mph that afternoon. The fire burned approximately 1285 acres.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A semi trailer with hay caught on fire east of LaPlant resulting in an approximately 290 acre burn that consumed mainly grassland. A peak wind gust of 25 mph was recorded at the nearby South Dakota Department of Transportation weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms erupted along a frontal boundary as a deep and negatively tilted trough lifted out of the central Rockies and approached the boundary during the late evening and overnight.  Storms initiated across central Nebraska, lifting northeast and quickly becoming linear with winds from 60 to 65 mph common from south central to east central South Dakota during the early overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gust measured by a personal weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong wake low developed beneath the stratiform area of a large convective complex across northwest Iowa, far southeast South Dakota, and southwest Minnesota. Winds commonly reached 60 to 72 mph and brought down numerous large limbs, powerlines, and damaged several buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained easterly winds reached 40 to 45 mph with gusts as high as 59 mph 3 miles south-southwest of Beresford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong negatively tilted trough lifted northeast through the Central Plains and resulted in the rapid intensification of low pressure as it moved from Kansas to northern Illinois. Conditions started on the warmer side with mainly light rain, drizzle, and a few thunderstorms on the morning of March 4, but a quick transition to snow occurred by evening and was accompanied by a drastic increase in winds to 45 to 60 mph. While snowfall ended up generally less than an inch across South Dakota, lack of existing snow cover prior to this system limited the areas for which the strong winds resulted in blizzard conditions. Much of southwest Minnesota, northwest Iowa, and far northeast Nebraska saw closed or impassible roads by the early morning of March 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 2.0 inches 4 miles north-northeast of Forestburg and near Letcher. Northerly winds sustained from 30 to 37 mph, which gusted as high as 52 mph near Forestburg, resulted in visibility briefly as low as one-half mile with hazardous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms erupted along a frontal boundary as a deep and negatively tilted trough lifted out of the central Rockies and approached the boundary during the late evening and overnight.  Storms initiated across central Nebraska, lifting northeast and quickly becoming linear with winds from 60 to 65 mph common from south central to east central South Dakota during the early overnight hours.

Redevelopment of scattered thunderstorms occurred along a frontal occlusion during the mid to late morning of May 15. Instability profiles were fairly shallow and strong, and low topped storms near the center of the upper-level low developed with enough shear to create enhanced outflow surges along the leading edge of the northward moving storms.  While a couple of funnel clouds were sighted, spotty damage was surveyed as resulting from thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds launched a trampoline and carried it for around one-half block and destroyed a portion of a residential fence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening area of low pressure across the Northern Plains on May 15 into early May 16 resulted in a period of very strong synoptic winds. The strongest winds were somewhat spotty, but a few gusts from 58 to 67 mph were recorded at times from late morning of the 15th into early morning on the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While the highest winds were inconsistent over the period, winds did reach sustained levels from 40 to 44 mph at times with gusts up to 62 mph at Wolsey and 61 mph at Huron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slightly elevated thunderstorms developed in the warm advection region, which featured steep mid-level lapse rates along an advancing inverted surface trough from Nebraska into southeast South Dakota during the afternoon and early evening.  The strongest isolated storms produced hail up to quarter size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-sized hail piled up on the ground about two inches deep.

Strong south winds, abnormally dry conditions and record temperatures over a 3 to 4 day period resulted in wildfires and blowing dust.||On Saturday, May 10th, temperatures topped out in the mid and upper 80s with peak wind gusts between 30 and 40 mph.  An issue with a train caused multiple small fires along 5 miles of track that parallels US Highway 14 in Hughes county, mainly near the town of Blunt. ||On Sunday, May 11th, temperatures topped out in the upper 80s to mid 90s, with record highs set in Sisseton and Mobridge. Wind gusts peaked at 60 mph at the Groton South Dakota State University mesonet station, with a vast majority of weather stations across the area seeing gusts top out over 50 mph.  Blowing dust was also evident on satellite across the James valley region, however in Hand county it was particularly notable with commuters and law enforcement reporting visibility down to zero at times. A fire developed on the edge of the town of Claremont and would go on to burn about 80 acres. Damage to power lines as a result of this fire would go on to cause an outage to 245 customers in Day county. A second fire, 6 miles southeast of Glenham would go on to burn 859 acres of mostly farm and pasture land, running north northeast for 3.25 miles. ||Monday, May 12th, was another day with strong winds of 45 to 55 mph, and temperatures topping out in the upper 80s to mid 90s. These strong winds resulted in zero visibility for portions of Hand county due to blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire developed from an old tree pile burn on 134th street and quickly spread north, mainly though pasture and field stubble. Damage included fence posts and power lines that ran through the area. Total acres burned was calculated at 859, with the fire running northeast for 3.25 miles. Fire suppression was accomplished by a combination of actions that included multiple fire agencies, including a Federal Aerial Drop Plane, with additional support from resident ranchers/farmers supplying tractors, discs and trailers with water tanks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system would generated high winds as it wrapped up overhead. A peak wind gust of 74 mph was observed by the South Dakota Department of Transportation mesonet station 6 miles south southwest of Peever. 3 additional reports of wind gusts at or in excess of 70 mph were noted, while 30 more weather stations would record wind gusts at or in excess of 60 mph. Numerous trees would lose limbs in the city of Aberdeen, with at least 3 downed trees being reported. These strong winds also caused a building to collapse in the town of Sisseton. Over a 36 hour period, 1425 customers would report the loss of power.

In advance of a deep mid-level trough pushing eastward out of the Rockies, a weakening line of thunderstorms moved northeast across the Northern Plains in the early morning hours. A large and dwindling stratiform area supported the development of a strong wake low with wind gusts as high as 70 to 75 mph in portions of southeast South Dakota as well as northwest Iowa. Sporadic tree and building damage were reported as well as numerous power outages, which left around 630 customers without power during the early morning hours along the Interstate 29 corridor in South Dakota and almost 1600 without power in parts of northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained easterly winds reached 30 to 39 mph with gusts as high as 56 mph near Dixon and 51 mph near Dallas.

A large and dwindling stratiform area supported the development of a strong wake low with wind gusts as high as 70 to 75 mph in portions of southeast South Dakota as well as northwest Iowa. Sporadic tree and building damage were reported as well as numerous power outages, which left around 630 customers without power during the early morning hours along the Interstate 29 corridor in South Dakota and almost 1600 without power in parts of northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained easterly winds reached 30 to 34 mph with gusts as high as 54 mph near Woonsocket and 51 mph near Letcher.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system would generated high winds as it wrapped up overhead. A peak wind gust of 74 mph was observed by the South Dakota Department of Transportation mesonet station 6 miles south southwest of Peever. 3 additional reports of wind gusts at or in excess of 70 mph were noted, while 30 more weather stations would record wind gusts at or in excess of 60 mph. Numerous trees would lose limbs in the city of Aberdeen, with at least 3 downed trees being reported. These strong winds also caused a building to collapse in the town of Sisseton. Over a 36 hour period, 1425 customers would report the loss of power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More seasonal rainfall was observed in middle to late May across north central South Dakota. A local area of moderate to heavy rain, between 4 to 6 inches, was observed with a system on May 15th and 16th, with the COOP observer located 8 miles south southwest of Keldron recording 5.6 inches of moisture. This would result in minor flooding of the Grand River, with the gauge near Little Eagle topping out at 15.43 feet, which is 0.43 feet above flood stage. Minor flooding of pastureland was the only impact observed with this rise in the river. The river would fall below flood stage within 24 hours, however another round of moisture would cause a second crest, this time at 0.48 feet above flood stage, on the 21st of May. The COOP observer 8 miles south southeast of Keldron would record an additional 2 inches of moisture leading up to this second crest. During this time, Oak Creek near Wakpala would also flood, reaching a peak of 11.51, or 1.51 feet above flood stage on May 21st. This would also only lead to minor flooding of nearby pasture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong negatively tilted trough lifted northeast through the Central Plains and resulted in the rapid intensification of low pressure as it moved from Kansas to northern Illinois. Conditions started on the warmer side with mainly light rain, drizzle, and a few thunderstorms on the morning of March 4, but a quick transition to snow occurred by evening and was accompanied by a drastic increase in winds to 45 to 60 mph. While snowfall ended up generally less than an inch across South Dakota, lack of existing snow cover prior to this system limited the areas for which the strong winds resulted in blizzard conditions. Much of southwest Minnesota, northwest Iowa, and far northeast Nebraska saw closed or impassible roads by the early morning of March 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusted up to 58 mph near Gayville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms erupted along a frontal boundary as a deep and negatively tilted trough lifted out of the central Rockies and approached the boundary during the late evening and overnight.  Storms initiated across central Nebraska, lifting northeast and quickly becoming linear with winds from 60 to 65 mph common from south central to east central South Dakota during the early overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees blown down in Geddes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weakening rain showers brought a brief period of gusty winds, which peaked at 58 mph 7 miles south-southwest of Gregory and 57 mph 2 miles east of Dixon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-moving, somewhat moisture starved upper level storm system moved across the Northern Plains February 7-8, producing 2 to 4 inches of snow across portions of far southeast South Dakota into adjacent portions of southwest Minnesota. While winds were not particularly strong, occasional wind gusts from 20 to 30 mph produced some patchy blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 2.5 inches 2 miles southeast of Madison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic airmass and elevated winds resulted in dangerous wind chills across parts of east-central South Dakota during the early morning of January 19. After a brief reprieve during the day, a secondary push of cold air resulted in the expansion of dangerous wind chills across much of southeast South Dakota January 20 and then extended into the morning hours of January 21 for some areas. The lowest wind chills during these periods ranged from 35 to 45 below zero with at least two locations dipping as low as 46 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The lowest wind chills ranged from 35 to 45 below zero with 45 below zero observed at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site near Howard.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The lowest wind chills ranged from 35 to 45 below zero with 45 below zero observed at the Madison Municipal Airport (KMDS).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A woman was found deceased just outside the city limits of Sioux Falls during the evening of January 19. The cause of death was exposure to the cold weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A deceased woman was found outdoors on January 19 just outside the city limits of Sioux Falls to the north of Family Park. Law enforcement noted the cause of death was exposure to the cold weather. Wind chill values throughout the day generally ranged from 10 to 30 below zero at the Sioux Falls Regional Airport (KFSD).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving Arctic air mass settled across the Northern Plains for multiple days in mid-February, resulting in daily minimum air temperatures below zero. These temperatures combined with light winds to result in dangerous wind chills, mainly during the overnight and early morning hours. The lowest wind chills during these periods ranged from 35 to 45 below zero with at least one location dipping as low as 46 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The lowest wind chills ranged from 35 to 45 below zero with 45 below zero observed at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site near Alexandria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure, clearing skies, and fresh snow cover allowed air temperatures to drop well below zero during the overnight and early morning hours of February 13. When combined with light winds, dangerous wind chills developed, ranging from 35 to 44 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The lowest wind chills ranged from 35 to 37 below zero with 37 below zero observed at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site near Cavour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of June 19th, strong thunderstorms developed in north-central South Dakota, moving east-southeast and producing large hail. Hail up to two inches in diameter was reported six miles north of Onaka and in the town of Faulkton.||As the storms traveled east, the primary threat shifted from hail to wind. Peak wind gusts of 68 mph were measured at Mayfield Colony and 4 miles east-southeast of Lake Norden. The winds downed trees and tree limbs in parts of rural Clark and Hamlin counties, including the towns of Estelline and Vienna. A machine shed in Mayfield Colony was also damaged.||Chasers also noted a brief tornado up around Hillside Colony, though no damage was noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level disturbance from Canada moved southeast across the Dakotas into Minnesota February 10-11, resulting in periods of light to moderate snowfall. The highest snow totals, exceeding 6 inches in some places, fell along a narrow west to east band just south of Interstate 90 across southeast South Dakota, coincident with the strongest low level frontogenesis. An arctic air mass followed the snowfall, resulting in many locations reporting wind chill values of 25 below or colder the morning of February 11.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 7.3 inches 10.8 miles south of Emery, 6.6 inches 4.5 miles west-southwest of Ethan, and 2.7 inches at Alexandria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving Arctic air mass settled across the Northern Plains for multiple days in mid-February, resulting in daily minimum air temperatures below zero. These temperatures combined with light winds to result in dangerous wind chills, mainly during the overnight and early morning hours. The lowest wind chills during these periods ranged from 35 to 45 below zero with at least one location dipping as low as 46 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The lowest wind chills ranged from 35 to 45 below zero with 45 below zero observed at the Madison Municipal Airport (KMDS).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile atmospheric setup on the afternoon and evening of June 28th led to the development of several powerful supercell thunderstorms across far eastern South Dakota. These storms produced multiple tornadoes, including two that caused significant damage in Deuel County. The strongest tornado was rated as a high-end EF3. Storms also produced large hail and instances of flash flooding from heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief, short lived tornado touched down in an open field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of an earlier cold front passage, elevated instability up to 1500 J/kg lingered as cross-boundary flow on the low-level jet reinvigorated storms in a deeply sheared post-frontal environment. Storms in far southeast South Dakota and northeast Nebraska produced spotty large hail, with a more intense cluster of both hail (up to 2.5 inches diameter) and wind (to around 80 mph) producing storms moving through northwest Iowa during the couple hours after midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms erupted along a cold front and a post-frontal boundary during the afternoon and early evening of April 28. Steep mid-level lapse rates around 8C/km and 55 to 65 knots of deep-layer shear led to severe storm structures, which evolved toward smaller-scale linear features along strong outflow pools with 60 to 65 mph winds during the event. Post-frontal storms developed in an environment of up to 1000 J/kg MUCAPE which favored mainly large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was quarter size and slightly larger.

A line of thunderstorms developed on the nose of a low-level jet, just in advance of a cold front across the central Dakotas. Deep-layer shear of 30-40 knots was largely parallel to the boundary and storms rapidly became linear with spotty damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was mostly pea size, but some stones were as large as nickels.

West-northwest synoptic winds gusted between 45 and 55 mph across southeast South Dakota during the afternoon and early evening of April 14 within a post-frontal cold air advection regime. Scattered showers developed, helping to bring even stronger severe gusts to the surface at a few locations. Peak gusts at these locations ranged from 58 to 62 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Aided by rain showers, winds briefly gusted above 58 mph, with a peak gust of 62 mph at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site at the Platte-Winner bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic airmass and elevated winds resulted in dangerous wind chills across parts of east-central South Dakota during the early morning of January 19. After a brief reprieve during the day, a secondary push of cold air resulted in the expansion of dangerous wind chills across much of southeast South Dakota January 20 and then extended into the morning hours of January 21 for some areas. The lowest wind chills during these periods ranged from 35 to 45 below zero with at least two locations dipping as low as 46 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The lowest wind chills ranged from 35 to 45 below zero with 45 below zero observed at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site near Wolsey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts ahead of a system increased to about 40 mph, with temperatures warming into the 70s with low humidity resulted in tinder dry conditions. A fire developed along highway 212 and spread rapidly northwards encompassing approximately 700 acres. The fire would flare back up 6 days later and require air resources for monitoring and extinguishment.

The lowest wind chills during these periods ranged from 35 to 45 below zero with at least one location dipping as low as 46 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The lowest wind chills ranged from 35 to 45 below zero with 45 below zero observed at the South Dakota Mesonet site near Volga.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms produced several observed wind gusts in excess of 50 mph.  A peak wind gust of 68 mph was measured by the South Dakota Department of Transportation mesonet station 4 miles west northwest of Cresbard. Winds would be strong enough to damage several small trees on the south side of the city of Aberdeen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee observed 2 large limbs that had come down on the south side of town associated with thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of locally heavy rain across the Upper Big Sioux Basin resulted in downstream minor flooding along portions of the Middle Big Sioux River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River near Estelline rose above flood stage on the afternoon of August 6th, cresting around 0.47 feet above flood stage (1644.47 ft) on August 8th. The river then fell below flood stage on the evening of August 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of locally heavy rain across the Upper Big Sioux Basin resulted in downstream minor flooding along portions of the Middle Big Sioux River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River near Bruce rose above flood stage on the evening of August 7th, cresting around 0.47 feet above flood stage (8.47 ft) on August 8th. The river then fell below flood stage on the morning of August 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall locally from 150 to 200 percent of normal during August alleviated the areas of worst drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions improved to moderate (D1) mid month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High levels of moisture pooled along and ahead of an outflow boundary across eastern South Dakota and southwest Minnesota with dew point readings into the the upper 70s to lower 80s.  With temperatures in the upper 80s to lower 90s, heat index values generally reached the 100 to 110 degree range during the mid afternoon to early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index values peaked as high as 104 degrees 2 miles north of Bonesteel and 100 degrees at Fairfax and Forth Randall Dam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two upper level waves moved across the Central and Northern Plains April 1 and 2 with multiple rounds of rain, snow, or a rain/snow mix. Initial warm advective precipitation moved across southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota the morning of April 1, followed by a relative lull until more substantial wrap-around precipitation blossomed April 2 as a surface low pressure system lifted from the near the Nebraska/Kansas border into south-central Minnesota and western Wisconsin. The majority of precipitation along and south of I-90 was rain, but cooler temperatures farther north allowed for some snow accumulation with the highest amounts along the Highway 14 corridor. Two-day snowfall generally ranged from 2 to 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 4.7 inches in Huron and 3.0 inches 2 miles southeast of Wessington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overnight severe weather produces a tornado in Rosebud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down 2 miles SE of Spring Creek just south of|BIA route #30 and remained over open field. The tornado tracked to|the east-northeast where it destroyed an outbuilding that was|built with heavy wood and anchored to a cement slab. The|outbuilding contents where thrown some 50yds to east up a hill.|The tornado crossed BIA route #30 where it strengthened,|destroying a single wide mobile home. The undercarriage of the|mobile home was tossed and crumpled 100yds from its foundation.|All walls where completely destroyed and the contents of the home|were thrown to the north to northeast. The tornado then moved |back into mostly open terrain before encountering some trees and |power poles just to the southwest of St. Francis. The circulation |then moved into St. Francis causing significant tree damage with |trees both uprooted and some trees near the catholic church had |their trunks snapped at the base. As the tornado passed through |the west side of St. Francis it caused significant roof damage to |the community center and an elderly living facility. The tornado |began to weaken slightly as it exited St. Francis and began to |move more toward the north-northeast and back over open terrain. |The tornado produced its last damage in Rosebud with the water |supply center and the adjacent storage facility receiving |signficant roof damage with the roofs being thrown toward the west|to southwest. The tornado dissipated 2 miles north of Rosebud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overnight severe weather produces a tornado in Rosebud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief spin-up touched down and produced EF0 damage to a|homestead 7 miles north of Mission. A mobile home lost all of its|roof and its north facing wall while the main home on the property|suffered major roof damage with most of the contents from the home|thrown back to the west. Other outbuildings on the property|suffered minor damage.

A system to the south looped up into Minnesota, generating a band of heavy snow and strong winds across mainly northeast South Dakota, with a lull and partial melting before wrap around precipitation and a re-freeze resulted in additional ice and snowfall.  Heavy snow and ice created slick roads and at times near-blizzard conditions. This led to a vehicle collision and fatality near the town of Milbank, while another fatality occurred on icy roads in Hand County. In Day County, icy roads caused a driver to slide into a recently opened pond. Numerous additional slide-ins and vehicle accidents were documented during this event. Snowfall amounts topped out at 12 inches in Waubay and Summit, 11 inches in Milbank and 10 inches at the National Weather Service in Aberdeen.

Drought conditions covered much of central, north central and northeast South Dakota at the start of the month. Mobridge, Pierre and Aberdeen would see near to slightly above normal precipitation, with Mobridge recording 1.92 inches, or 0.34 inches above normal, Pierre 2.21 inches or 0.28 inches above normal, while Aberdeen would observer 3.07 inches, or 1.16 inches above normal for April.  Temperatures for April were also near-normal. This moisture would reduce the area of severe drought by the end of the month.

A low pressure system behind a thunderstorm complex generated sporadic high winds across central South Dakota. A peak wind gust of 69 mph was recorded at the South Dakota Department of Transportation mesonet site 3 miles southeast of Reliance, with a 67 mph gust recorded at the South Dakota State University mesonet station 6 miles south southeast of Kennebec.

A peak gust of 64 mph was recorded at the Remote Automatic Weather Station 10 miles south of Bullhead, while the South Dakota Department of Transportation weather stations 5 miles southeast of Selby and in Ridgeview would record a peak gust of 62 mph and 60 mph respectively.

A clipper system would generate strong winds during the afternoon and evening of April 9th. A system to the south looped up into Minnesota, generating a band of heavy snow and strong winds across mainly northeast South Dakota, with a lull and partial melting before wrap around precipitation and a re-freeze resulted in additional ice and snowfall.  Heavy snow and ice created slick roads and at times near-blizzard conditions. This led to a vehicle collision and fatality near the town of Milbank, while another fatality occurred on icy roads in Hand County. In Day County, icy roads caused a driver to slide into a recently opened pond. Numerous additional slide-ins and vehicle accidents were documented during this event. Snowfall amounts topped out at 12 inches in Waubay and Summit, 11 inches in Milbank and 10 inches at the National Weather Service in Aberdeen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A two vehicle accident resulted in a fatality near the town of Milbank in near blizzard conditions, with the nearby Automated Weather Observing System recording a visibility of 1/4 miles during the accident. The COOP observer in Milbank would also record 11 inches of total snow for the system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure spread from the Central Plains early on March 14 to central Minnesota by the morning of March 15. Very strong winds developed on the back side of the system, with sustained winds from 30 to 43 mph and spotty gusts as high as 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 42 mph at times near Plankinton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong negatively tilted trough lifted northeast through the Central Plains and resulted in the rapid intensification of low pressure as it moved from Kansas to northern Illinois. Conditions started on the warmer side with mainly light rain, drizzle, and a few thunderstorms on the morning of March 4, but a quick transition to snow occurred by evening and was accompanied by a drastic increase in winds to 45 to 60 mph. While snowfall ended up generally less than an inch across South Dakota, lack of existing snow cover prior to this system limited the areas for which the strong winds resulted in blizzard conditions. Much of southwest Minnesota, northwest Iowa, and far northeast Nebraska saw closed or impassible roads by the early morning of March 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 1.0 inch at De Smet. Northerly winds from 30 to 36 mph, which gusted as high as 46 mph at De Smet, resulted in visibility briefly as low as one-quarter to one-half mile with hazardous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong negatively tilted trough lifted northeast through the Central Plains and resulted in the rapid intensification of low pressure as it moved from Kansas to northern Illinois. Conditions started on the warmer side with mainly light rain, drizzle, and a few thunderstorms on the morning of March 4, but a quick transition to snow occurred by evening and was accompanied by a drastic increase in winds to 45 to 60 mph. While snowfall ended up generally less than an inch across South Dakota, lack of existing snow cover prior to this system limited the areas for which the strong winds resulted in blizzard conditions. Much of southwest Minnesota, northwest Iowa, and far northeast Nebraska saw closed or impassible roads by the early morning of March 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 0.7 inches at Canton and 0.4 inches at Harrisburg.  Northerly winds sustained around 40 mph with gusts as high as 57 mph occurred 7 miles east of Davis and resulted in visibility briefly as low as one-half mile with hazardous travel conditions.  Due to the strong crosswinds and slick road conditions, a driver was injured when a vehicle traveling east on 294th Street 4 miles east of Interstate 29 slid off the highway and rolled into a creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure spread from the Central Plains early on March 14 to central Minnesota by the morning of March 15. Very strong winds developed on the back side of the system, with sustained winds from 30 to 43 mph and spotty gusts as high as 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 43 mph at times at Dallas, 7 miles south-southwest of Gregory, and 16 miles north of Gregory with a gusts as high as 57 mph, and a gust as high as 61 mph at Fort Randall Dam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite an overall warm and wet month, drought status remained largely unchanged during March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions persisted through the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of heavy rain across northeast South Dakota resulted in downstream river gauges within the upper and middle Big Sioux River Basin to rise into Minor and locally Moderate flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper-level pattern experienced amplification of an eastern U.S. ridge and western U.S. trough, which locked a low-level frontal boundary in place from western Nebraska through central South Dakota into the Great Lakes. This allowed for heat to spread northward as very high dew point readings pooled across the area with the recent wet conditions. This multi-day dangerous heat event featured daytime heat index readings from 100 to 120 degrees, with nighttime low temperatures remaining from the mid 70s to lower 80s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daily afternoon and evening heat indices reached around 105 degrees with a maximum heat index of 105 degrees at the Road Weather Information System site near Kimball on June 20.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed in the wake of earlier storms in a purely elevated environment, with an isolated stronger storm resulting in penny size hail.

Weak warm advection initiated isolated convection in a low-shear, high CAPE environment. Slow moving storms made the most of strong initial updrafts to produce hail up to half-dollar size, followed by damaging wind gusts later in storm evolution.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rounds of severe weather spawned large hail (4.5) and a confirmed tornado across western South Dakota. Severe storms also produced severe wind gusts across the eastern Plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down 100 yards west of the Highway 73 White|River Bridge. As the tornado moved to the southeast in the river|valley, the first residence that suffered significant damage was|just east of Highway 73, on County Road 29. This home suffered|partial exterior wall collapse, with most of the roof structure|gone. Additionally, mature Cottonwood trees were snapped at|this location. As the tornado continued to move east-southeast|down the river valley, it encountered house number 2, also on|County Road 29. Several outbuildings were destroyed at this|location, along with mature Cottonwood trees that were|snapped and partially debarked. The 3rd residence, located along|County Road 80, or Red Stone Rd, was a new manufactured home that|suffered complete destruction. Two individuals were in the home at|the time and suffered minor injuries. The home was strapped down,|with the straps anchored in concrete. It is estimated the house|rolled 3 times, with the house separating from the frame. The|frame became partially wrapped around the trunk of a fallen|Cottonwood tree. There were numerous mature Cottonwood trees|snapped and partially debarked, as well as uprooted, on this|property. The debris from the house was blown to the north through|northeast toward the White River. The tornado continued moving|east-southeast in the river valley, snapping and debarking mature|Cottonwood trees before it lifted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the midst of extremely warm air aloft and 9 to 9.5 degrees C/km mid-level lapse rates, a weak cluster of thunderstorms developed and moved into the Sioux Falls metro area during the early morning hours.  Exceedingly great values of DCAPE aided development of strong downdrafts, with the storm showing the signals of a modified convective heat burst in portions of the city away from the greater precipitation core with damage in isolated spots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind was measured at Joe Foss Field in Sioux Falls. A large pine tree was snapped off at a nearby golf course less than one-half mile southwest of the ASOS site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple clusters of storms moved across portions of central, north central and northeast South Dakota on the morning of July 23rd. Many of these storms produced strong winds and wind related damage. A peak wind gust of 80 mph was observed by the South Dakota State University mesonet station near Lower Brule, while a peak wind gust of 76 mph was measured by the South Dakota Department of Transportation weather station two miles south of Lowry. Thunderstorm winds were also responsible for damage grain bins and the Agtegra building near the town of Carpenter. Three boaters required rescue when their boat capsized on Dry Lake due to thunderstorm winds.

Thunderstorms erupted within a very moist and unstable environment across central South Dakota during the afternoon of July 29 in response to an approaching upper level short-wave from Colorado. These thunderstorms quickly grew upscale into a line, producing 55 to 75 mph wind gusts across southeast South Dakota, resulting in isolated tree damage. In addition, prolonged heavy rain resulted in some localized flash flooding across northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts were measured by a personal weather station.

A west-to-east oriented broken line of thunderstorms along the South Dakota-Nebraska border near sunrise on July 23 rapidly lifted north the remainder of the morning in response to a strong low-level jet. Damaging wind gusts were observed across portions of Aurora and Beadle Counties with the Huron Regional Airport (KHON) reporting a wind gust of 82 mph. Tree damage was reported across Huron and Plankinton, South Dakota with isolated structural damage in other parts of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several 3-to-4-inch diameter tree branches across Plankinton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms erupted within a very moist and unstable environment across central South Dakota during the afternoon of July 29 in response to an approaching upper level short-wave from Colorado. These thunderstorms quickly grew upscale into a line, producing 55 to 75 mph wind gusts across southeast South Dakota, resulting in isolated tree damage. In addition, prolonged heavy rain resulted in some localized flash flooding across northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts blew down 8-9 inch diameter healthy tree limbs.

On the evening of June 19th, strong thunderstorms developed in north-central South Dakota, moving east-southeast and producing large hail. Hail up to two inches in diameter was reported six miles north of Onaka and in the town of Faulkton.||As the storms traveled east, the primary threat shifted from hail to wind. Peak wind gusts of 68 mph were measured at Mayfield Colony and 4 miles east-southeast of Lake Norden. The winds downed trees and tree limbs in parts of rural Clark and Hamlin counties, including the towns of Estelline and Vienna. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High heat and humidity settled into the region between June 20th and 22nd. A high temperature of 106 degrees was observed by a mesonet station 2 miles south southwest of Fort Pierre, with 105 degrees observed by the COOP in the town of Kennebec on June 20th. 22 additional weather stations, mainly across central South Dakota, would top out at or just above the century mark. Thanks to dewpoints well into the 60s and low 70s, heat index values at or above 100 were quite widespread, with a mesonet station in Mellette topping out at 117, while Onaka, Stockholm and Straford would record peak heat index value of 115 degrees.||Conditions did not improve on Saturday June 21st, with the core of heat shifting east. A high temperature of 102 was observed by mesonet stations in the city of Watertown, as well as at the South Dakota State University mesonet station at the Redfield Municipal Airport. Heat index values Saturday would top out at 118 in the town of Stratford and 116 in the town of Castlewood. ||The heat was a little less intense Sunday, June 22nd. While weather stations in the area failed to top 100 degrees, heat index values of 113 degrees were observed in the town of Castlewood and 112 degrees in the town of Mellette.
