EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused water to run over South Dakota Highway 27 one mile south of  Pierpont. Water was also reported over 422nd ave, 3 miles east of Pierpont, where a vehicle hydroplaned into the ditch. One of the rural county roads 5 miles southeast of Pierpont was partly washed out. Roadside ditches were full across the better part of northwest and north central Day county, with water overtopping several additional roads. 3.6 inches of rainfall was measured 6 miles east southeast of Pierpont by a resident, while a trained spotter 1 mile south of Roslyn recorded 4.64 inches. A resident 7 miles west of Roslyn would go on to estimate they received 5 inches of total rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two clusters of storms would move across the forecast area on the 13th into the early morning hours of the 14th. The day started with an isolated stronger storm generating 1 inch hail 6 miles north northeast of Lowry. That cluster would continue to move east and intensify, with 1.5 inch hail observed 8 miles east of Pierpont. These storms would damage siding and crops around Pierpont, with additional thunderstorm wind damage to trees in the town of Roslyn. ||Another cluster of storms would move through central South Dakota, with a peak wind gust of 73 mph recorded by the South Dakota State University mesonet station 12 miles south of Vivian. As this cluster departed and weakened, additional winds were generated, with a gust of 68 mph 3 miles southeast of Reliance on a South Dakota Department of Transportation mesonet station.||A total of 3.6 inches was observed 6 miles east southeast of Pierpont. This rainfall, on top of already saturated soils from the previous month's heavy rains, resulted in water over roads, a partial washout, and at least one stranded vehicle in this area. In Roslyn, a trained spotter recorded 4.64 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 73 mph was recorded at a South Dakota State University mesonet station 12 miles south of Vivian.

High levels of moisture pooled along and ahead of an outflow boundary across eastern South Dakota and southwest Minnesota with dew point readings into the the upper 70s to lower 80s.  With temperatures in the upper 80s to lower 90s, heat index values generally reached the 100 to 110 degree range during the mid afternoon to early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index values peaked as high as 101 degrees near Platte and 3 miles west of Geddes, and 100 degrees 4 miles east-southeast of Wagner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak wave lifting out of the central Rockies aided in the initiation of a forward propagating Mesoscale Convective System from the lee of the Black Hills southward into the Nebraska Panhandle. With abundant MUCAPE up to nearly 4000 J/kg and DCAPE up to 1500 J/kg, but deep-layer shear only around 20-25 knots, storms retained a somewhat outflow dominant character while on an east-northeast trek. Winds were generally from 60 to 80 mph with significant damage to trees and a few structures along the track of the system north of Interstate 90 into southwest Minnesota. Around 600 customers were without power across Jerauld, Sanborn and Kingsbury Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While most hailstones were smaller, a few were up to golf ball size.

Additional sporadic tree and crop damage was noted elsewhere across northeast South Dakota. ||Heavy rainfall would also lead to flooding with reports of several roads overtopped by water in the city of Watertown, the towns of Britton, Agency Village, and Wilmot, as well as portions of rural Codington, Roberts, and Marshall counties. These heavy rains would result in river flooding, mainly across the Big Sioux and its tributaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail accompanied thunderstorm winds resulting in crop and garden produce damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heat and moisture pooled near and south of a nearly stationary frontal boundary across eastern South Dakota and southern Minnesota.  Daily highs were in the mid 80s to lower 90s with dew point values in the upper 70s to lower 80s, yielding heat index values from 100 to 115 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerous heat index values peaked as high as 105 degrees at Wagner on August 15 and 8 miles east of Wagner on August 16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak wave lifting out of the central Rockies aided in the initiation of a forward propagating Mesoscale Convective System from the lee of the Black Hills southward into the Nebraska Panhandle. With abundant MUCAPE up to nearly 4000 J/kg and DCAPE up to 1500 J/kg, but deep-layer shear only around 20-25 knots, storms retained a somewhat outflow dominant character while on an east-northeast trek. Winds were generally from 60 to 80 mph with significant damage to trees and a few structures along the track of the system north of Interstate 90 into southwest Minnesota. Around 600 customers were without power across Jerauld, Sanborn and Kingsbury Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts were measured by a personal weather station 4 miles southwest of Chamberlain.

Brief but intense heavy rainfall from thunderstorms, on top of weeks of wet weather, resulted in additional flooding across Marshall, Hamlin, Codington, and Deuel counties starting on the 17th and persisting well into the 18th.  An estimated 3 inches of rain fell in Lake Poinsett, while 1.63 inches was recorded by the COOP sensor 20 miles north northeast of Aberdeen, 1.62 inches was submitted by the CoCoRaHS volunteer in Claremont, and 1.5 inches by the CoCoRaHS observer in Hayti. Flooding was most prominent in urban areas such as Aberdeen where multiple roads and intersections flooded. Clear Lake and Lake Poinsett would also see significant water issues.||These storms would also generate sporadic strong winds, with a gust of 63 mph recorded at the Aberdeen Regional Airport.  A 61 mph gust was recorded by the South Dakota Department of Transportation mesonet station 4 miles west of Trail City, and a gust to 62 mph at the South Dakota State University mesonet station near La Plant.

With abundant MUCAPE up to nearly 4000 J/kg and DCAPE up to 1500 J/kg, but deep-layer shear only around 20-25 knots, storms retained a somewhat outflow dominant character while on an east-northeast trek. Winds were generally from 60 to 80 mph with significant damage to trees and a few structures along the track of the system north of Interstate 90 into southwest Minnesota. Around 600 customers were without power across Jerauld, Sanborn and Kingsbury Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts were measured by a Road Weather Information System sensor 5 miles west of Plankinton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms produced large hail and high winds during the afternoon and evening of August 5th, and into the early morning hours of August 6th. The largest sized hail measured at 3 inches in diameter was observed in Hoven. Later in the evening, a peak wind gust of 99 mph was recorded on a weather station out at Sandy Shore State Park, while the Watertown Regional Airport ASOS would record a peak gust of 68 mph. Over 40 trees were topped or downed as a result of these winds around the Lake Kampeska area. Additional sporadic tree and crop damage was noted elsewhere across northeast South Dakota. ||Heavy rainfall would also lead to flooding with reports of several roads overtopped by water in the city of Watertown, the towns of Britton, Agency Village, and Wilmot, as well as portions of rural Codington, Roberts, and Marshall counties. Already saturated soils from a wet spring and summer were unable to absorb the 2 to 4 inches of rainfall, with the COOP observer in Wilmot measuring 3.63 inches and 3.31 inches of rainfall from a CoCoRaHS observer 1 mile south southeast of Kidder. These heavy rains would result in river flooding, mainly across the Big Sioux and its tributaries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Brief but intense heavy rainfall from thunderstorms, on top of weeks of wet weather, resulted in additional flooding across Marshall, Hamlin, Codington, and Deuel counties starting on the 17th and persisting well into the 18th.  An estimated 3 inches of rain fell in Lake Poinsett, while 1.63 inches was recorded by the COOP sensor 20 miles north northeast of Aberdeen, 1.62 inches was submitted by the CoCoRaHS volunteer in Claremont, and 1.5 inches by the CoCoRaHS observer in Hayti. Flooding was most prominent in urban areas such as Aberdeen where multiple roads and intersections flooded. Clear Lake and Lake Poinsett would also see significant water issues.||These storms would also generate sporadic strong winds, with a gust of 63 mph recorded at the Aberdeen Regional Airport.  A 61 mph gust was recorded by the South Dakota Department of Transportation mesonet station 4 miles west of Trail City, and a gust to 62 mph at the South Dakota State University mesonet station near La Plant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cumulative heavy rainfall from May through mid-August, along with reservoir releases in North Dakota, resulted in flooding along the James River. Impacts from the flooding were limited to agricultural lands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river near Columbia went into flood at 9:15 p.m. CST on August 10th and remained in flood through the rest of the month. It crested at 15.15 feet, which is 2.15 feet above flood stage, primarily impacting agricultural lands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong storms moved through northeastern South Dakota during the evening of August 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Dakota Department of Transportation mesonet station in Turnerville topped out at 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two clusters of storms would move across the forecast area on the 13th into the early morning hours of the 14th. The day started with an isolated stronger storm generating 1 inch hail 6 miles north northeast of Lowry. That cluster would continue to move east and intensify, with 1.5 inch hail observed 8 miles east of Pierpont. These storms would damage siding and crops around Pierpont, with additional thunderstorm wind damage to trees in the town of Roslyn. ||Another cluster of storms would move through central South Dakota, with a peak wind gust of 73 mph recorded by the South Dakota State University mesonet station 12 miles south of Vivian. This rainfall, on top of already saturated soils from the previous month's heavy rains, resulted in water over roads, a partial washout, and at least one stranded vehicle in this area. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall on saturated soils resulted in flooding in and around Watertown, with street flooding reported across the city. Northeast of Watertown, a vehicle left the Interstate after encountering some 10 inches of water on the roadway that flooded both  lanes near mile marker 182. When emergency crews arrived, the car was barely visible, with only the rear bumper above water. The occupant of the car swam to safety, was checked out on the scene by paramedics, but did not require any further medical attention.|Due to flooding, Interstate 29 was shut down between Exit 181 and Exit 185.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Brief but intense heavy rainfall from thunderstorms, on top of weeks of wet weather, resulted in additional flooding across Marshall, Hamlin, Codington, and Deuel counties starting on the 17th and persisting well into the 18th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms moved over portions of northeast South Dakota that had really no extra room for moisture from the preceding months of nearly constant rainfall. Thus, this moisture went straight into the Big Sioux and tributaries from the Watertown area down south to Castlewood. The COOP observer in Rauville would record 1.51 inches of rainfall pushing their monthly total up to 5.73 and a 3 month total of 23.32. The Watertown COOP would likewise come up with 1.86 inches, putting the monthly total at 5.85 and 20.1 for the preceding 3 months. Even Castlewood, while only topping out at 0.31 inches, would see their monthly total top out at 4.99 inches with a 3 month total of 22 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Branch Tributary Mud Creek near Rauville went into flood during the early morning hours of August 22nd, cresting at 4.91 feet, 0.91 feet above flood stage around mid-day. The creek would remain in flood through the early afternoon of August 23rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River near Estelline rose above flood stage on the morning of August 17th, cresting around 2.53 feet above flood stage (1646.53 ft) on August 19th. This crest is in the moderate flood category. The river then fell below flood stage on the morning of August 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak wave lifting out of the central Rockies aided in the initiation of a forward propagating Mesoscale Convective System from the lee of the Black Hills southward into the Nebraska Panhandle. With abundant MUCAPE up to nearly 4000 J/kg and DCAPE up to 1500 J/kg, but deep-layer shear only around 20-25 knots, storms retained a somewhat outflow dominant character while on an east-northeast trek. Winds were generally from 60 to 80 mph with significant damage to trees and a few structures along the track of the system north of Interstate 90 into southwest Minnesota. Around 600 customers were without power across Jerauld, Sanborn and Kingsbury Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts caused extensive tree damage throughout the De Smet city limits, but no structural damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms produced large hail and high winds during the afternoon and evening of August 5th, and into the early morning hours of August 6th. The largest sized hail measured at 3 inches in diameter was observed in Hoven. Later in the evening, a peak wind gust of 99 mph was recorded on a weather station out at Sandy Shore State Park, while the Watertown Regional Airport ASOS would record a peak gust of 68 mph. Over 40 trees were topped or downed as a result of these winds around the Lake Kampeska area. Additional sporadic tree and crop damage was noted elsewhere across northeast South Dakota. ||Heavy rainfall would also lead to flooding with reports of several roads overtopped by water in the city of Watertown, the towns of Britton, Agency Village, and Wilmot, as well as portions of rural Codington, Roberts, and Marshall counties. Already saturated soils from a wet spring and summer were unable to absorb the 2 to 4 inches of rainfall, with the COOP observer in Wilmot measuring 3.63 inches and 3.31 inches of rainfall from a CoCoRaHS observer 1 mile south southeast of Kidder. These heavy rains would result in river flooding, mainly across the Big Sioux and its tributaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A caller stated that multiple large tree branches had come down at Lee Park golf course.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two clusters of storms would move across the forecast area on the 13th into the early morning hours of the 14th. The day started with an isolated stronger storm generating 1 inch hail 6 miles north northeast of Lowry. That cluster would continue to move east and intensify, with 1.5 inch hail observed 8 miles east of Pierpont. These storms would damage siding and crops around Pierpont, with additional thunderstorm wind damage to trees in the town of Roslyn. ||Another cluster of storms would move through central South Dakota, with a peak wind gust of 73 mph recorded by the South Dakota State University mesonet station 12 miles south of Vivian. As this cluster departed and weakened, additional winds were generated, with a gust of 68 mph 3 miles southeast of Reliance on a South Dakota Department of Transportation mesonet station.||A total of 3.6 inches was observed 6 miles east southeast of Pierpont. This rainfall, on top of already saturated soils from the previous month's heavy rains, resulted in water over roads, a partial washout, and at least one stranded vehicle in this area. In Roslyn, a trained spotter recorded 4.64 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A caller stated that winds with the storms gusted to between 50 and 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system moved across South Dakota into northwest Minnesota on October 20, allowing a strong cold front to sweep across the region. Surface winds shifted to northwest behind the front, gusting at times between 40 and 55 mph. A couple locations across south-central South Dakota briefly reported sustained winds in excess of 40 mph with gusts to 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds briefly reached 40 to 42 mph with a peak wind gust of 58 mph at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site near the Platte-Winner Bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low-pressure system zipped across northeast South Dakota, resulting in powerful northwest winds. Widespread wind gusts in excess of 58 mph were reported, with 55 total reports of high winds associated with this system. The highest recorded gust reached 77 mph at the South Dakota Department of Transportation (SDDOT) weather station in Clark. The Aberdeen Regional Airport ASOS recorded a peak gust of 74 mph. The winds caused significant damage in Aberdeen, including blown-over trees, fence damage, and shattered windows at a downtown business. Power outages affected 4,267 residents across the region, including 1,791 in Brown County and 1,192 in Spink County.

A strong low-pressure system and a blast of arctic air produced high winds and pockets of localized blizzard conditions across the region. The highest wind gust of 83 mph was recorded by the South Dakota Department of Transportation (SDDOT) mesonet station near Ridgeview, SD and 81 mph winds were recorded by the SDDOT mesonet station 9 miles north northwest of Vivian, SD. 26 additional weather stations would record a peak wind gust in excess of 70 mph.  High winds and light snow resulted in pockets of intermittent blizzard conditions.||High winds were responsible for blowing in the top of a grain bin in Murdo, toppling a small steel building under construction in Timber Lake, and causing a semi to jackknife near the town of Frederick in Brown County. Another semi closed portions of Highway 28 in Hamlin county after it was blown into the ditch.  Roberts, Marshall and Day counties were placed under a No Travel Advised for the duration of the system. ||Several power outages were also noted, totaling of 1647 residents, including 355 residents in Marshall and 322 residents in McPherson counties.  Day, Spink, Lyman and Stanley all suffered power infrastructure damage due to these high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic, record-breaking wind storm wreaked havoc across northeastern Wyoming and western South Dakota on December 17-18, 2025. Wind gusts reached 101 MPH, resulting in significant damage to trees, power lines, and some structures. A couple of fires were also propelled by the high winds. All-time high wind gusts were officially recorded at the National Weather Service in east Rapid City, SD (91 MPH at 4:56 AM MST on December 18) and the Rapid City Regional Airport (94 MPH at 5:13 AM MST on December 18).||Rapidly deepening low-pressure system over Saskatchewan on December 17 tracked east-southeastward into northern Minnesota on December 18. The low-pressure system dragged a warm front through the area on December 17, producing strong, gusty southwest winds up to 77 MPH in its wake. The passage of the low pushed the first cold front into the region late in the afternoon on December 17, bringing a period of strong northwest winds lasting several hours. The second, and most potent, cold front moved through the area from late evening on December 17 into the morning of December 18. Following the passage of the second cold front, historic  wind gusts were observed, persisting for 4 to 8 hours.||Some residents in the Black Hills experienced power outages that lasted a week. Further details are available in the tabs below.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated wind gust at 11 W Hot Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-moving low pressure system dropped southeastward across the Northern Plains on December 6. Light to moderate snow resulted in widespread accumulations of 2 to 6 inches. Snowfall rates ranged from 1 to 2 inches per hour within a narrow band across far southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa where snowfall totaled 6 to 10 inches. Rapid snow accumulation resulted in slick and snow covered roads, making regional travel difficult. Some patchy freezing drizzle fell as well after the bulk of the snow moved out of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 4.5 inches at De Smet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low-pressure system zipped across northeast South Dakota, resulting in powerful northwest winds. Widespread wind gusts in excess of 58 mph were reported, with 55 total reports of high winds associated with this system. The highest recorded gust reached 77 mph at the South Dakota Department of Transportation (SDDOT) weather station in Clark. The Aberdeen Regional Airport ASOS recorded a peak gust of 74 mph. The winds caused significant damage in Aberdeen, including blown-over trees, fence damage, and shattered windows at a downtown business. Power outages affected 4,267 residents across the region, including 1,791 in Brown County and 1,192 in Spink County.

A strong low-pressure system and a blast of arctic air produced high winds and pockets of localized blizzard conditions across the region. The highest wind gust of 83 mph was recorded by the South Dakota Department of Transportation (SDDOT) mesonet station near Ridgeview, SD and 81 mph winds were recorded by the SDDOT mesonet station 9 miles north northwest of Vivian, SD. 26 additional weather stations would record a peak wind gust in excess of 70 mph.  High winds and light snow resulted in pockets of intermittent blizzard conditions.||High winds were responsible for blowing in the top of a grain bin in Murdo, toppling a small steel building under construction in Timber Lake, and causing a semi to jackknife near the town of Frederick in Brown County. Another semi closed portions of Highway 28 in Hamlin county after it was blown into the ditch.  Roberts, Marshall and Day counties were placed under a No Travel Advised for the duration of the system. ||Several power outages were also noted, totaling of 1647 residents, including 355 residents in Marshall and 322 residents in McPherson counties.  Day, Spink, Lyman and Stanley all suffered power infrastructure damage due to these high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure moved across North Dakota and northern Minnesota early on December 18th. A very strong cold front plowed southward behind the system and was accompanied by a minimal amount of snowfall over several hours during the later morning and afternoon hours. While most snowfall was limited to a half inch to inch at most, increasing collocation of the snow and strong wind east of Interstate 29 resulted in areas of blizzard or near blizzard conditions across parts of southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa. Several injury accidents were reported across northwest Iowa with the Department of Transportation advising no travel for several hours in the afternoon. The high winds also produced some spotty power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds were sustained between 40 and 52 mph at times, with gusts as high as 63 mph at Yankton and 2 miles west-southwest of Gayville, and 58 mph 10 miles north of Yankton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent winter storm moved from Kansas northeastward to the Great Lakes on December 28 into the morning hours of December 29.  The system brought a leading and more prominent band of snowfall during the morning and midday on December 28, followed by a break in precipitation.  As the system deepened during the evening, the band of snowfall wrapped back southward through far eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa.  When the current and previous snow combined with winds gusting from 40 to 60 mph, widespread blizzard conditions developed from southwest Minnesota into northwest Iowa.  State Departments of Transportation advised no travel across southern Minnesota and portions of Iowa from late morning on the 28th through mid-morning of the 29th.  Numerous roadways were closed due to numerous crashes.  Total snowfall reached as high as 2 to 5 inches in much of southwest Minnesota into northwest Iowa, with 1 to 2 inches along the Missouri River and Interstate 29 corridors and lesser amounts toward the mid James River valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Short duration near-blizzard conditions occurred, divided by a longer improvement in conditions as falling snow ended for a time during the afternoon and early evening. Gusty northwest winds from 35 to 55 mph were concurrent with light snowfall, which totaled 1.0 inch near Jefferson and 10 miles north-northeast of Elk Point, and resulted in brief visibility of around one-half mile that disrupted travel.

Snowfall rates ranged from 1 to 2 inches per hour within a narrow band across far southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa where snowfall totaled 6 to 10 inches. Rapid snow accumulation resulted in slick and snow covered roads, making regional travel difficult. Some patchy freezing drizzle fell as well after the bulk of the snow moved out of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 1.5 inches 3.8 miles southwest of Stickney and 1.2 inches at White Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low-pressure system zipped across northeast South Dakota, resulting in powerful northwest winds. Widespread wind gusts in excess of 58 mph were reported, with 55 total reports of high winds associated with this system. The highest recorded gust reached 77 mph at the South Dakota Department of Transportation (SDDOT) weather station in Clark. The Aberdeen Regional Airport ASOS recorded a peak gust of 74 mph. The winds caused significant damage in Aberdeen, including blown-over trees, fence damage, and shattered windows at a downtown business. Power outages affected 4,267 residents across the region, including 1,791 in Brown County and 1,192 in Spink County.

A strong low-pressure system and a blast of arctic air produced high winds and pockets of localized blizzard conditions across the region. The highest wind gust of 83 mph was recorded by the South Dakota Department of Transportation (SDDOT) mesonet station near Ridgeview, SD and 81 mph winds were recorded by the SDDOT mesonet station 9 miles north northwest of Vivian, SD. 26 additional weather stations would record a peak wind gust in excess of 70 mph.  High winds and light snow resulted in pockets of intermittent blizzard conditions.||High winds were responsible for blowing in the top of a grain bin in Murdo, toppling a small steel building under construction in Timber Lake, and causing a semi to jackknife near the town of Frederick in Brown County. Another semi closed portions of Highway 28 in Hamlin county after it was blown into the ditch.  Roberts, Marshall and Day counties were placed under a No Travel Advised for the duration of the system. ||Several power outages were also noted, totaling of 1647 residents, including 355 residents in Marshall and 322 residents in McPherson counties.  Day, Spink, Lyman and Stanley all suffered power infrastructure damage due to these high winds.

A historic, record-breaking wind storm wreaked havoc across northeastern Wyoming and western South Dakota on December 17-18, 2025. Wind gusts reached 101 MPH, resulting in significant damage to trees, power lines, and some structures. A couple of fires were also propelled by the high winds. All-time high wind gusts were officially recorded at the National Weather Service in east Rapid City, SD (91 MPH at 4:56 AM MST on December 18) and the Rapid City Regional Airport (94 MPH at 5:13 AM MST on December 18).||Rapidly deepening low-pressure system over Saskatchewan on December 17 tracked east-southeastward into northern Minnesota on December 18. The low-pressure system dragged a warm front through the area on December 17, producing strong, gusty southwest winds up to 77 MPH in its wake. The passage of the low pushed the first cold front into the region late in the afternoon on December 17, bringing a period of strong northwest winds lasting several hours. The second, and most potent, cold front moved through the area from late evening on December 17 into the morning of December 18. Following the passage of the second cold front, historic  wind gusts were observed, persisting for 4 to 8 hours.||Some residents in the Black Hills experienced power outages that lasted a week. Further details are available in the tabs below.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Dakota mesonet site at  11 W Milesville recorded wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Locally high winds developed on the evening of December 14th lasting into the early morning hours of December 15th. The South Dakota Department of Transportation mesonet 6 miles south southwest of Peever recorded a peak wind gust of 64 mph.

Locally high winds developed in the favored area near the Sisseton hills. The South Dakota Department of Transportation weather station 6 miles south southwest of Peever recorded a peak wind gust of 67 mph with winds at or in excess of 58 mph for a little over an hour.

Strong winds and light snow combined to produce blizzard conditions across far northeast South Dakota. The Milbank Automated Weather Observing System (AWOS) recorded a peak wind gust of 62 mph and consistent visibilities between 1/4 and 1/2 mile. The COOP observer in Webster recorded 3.2 inches of snow, while 2.5 inches fell near LaBolt. The hazardous conditions prompted No Travel Advised notices in Roberts and Day counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions reported on Milbank AWOS starting around 8 am with visibility consistently down to between a quarter to a half mile. Wind gusts were initially up around 45 mph, but increased through the day with a peak of 62 mph by 710pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and light snow combined to produce blizzard conditions across far northeast South Dakota. The Milbank Automated Weather Observing System (AWOS) recorded a peak wind gust of 62 mph and consistent visibilities between 1/4 and 1/2 mile. The COOP observer in Webster recorded 3.2 inches of snow, while 2.5 inches fell near LaBolt. The hazardous conditions prompted No Travel Advised notices in Roberts and Day counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Day County Sheriff issued No Travel Advised for the county due to snow showers and high winds reducing visibility on the roadways. These conditions resulted in a semi stuck in snow on Highway 12. Winds at the South Dakota State University mesonet weather station in Webster recorded a peak wind gust of 40 mph, while the COOP in Webster recorded a snow total of 3.2 inches.

All-time high wind gusts were officially recorded at the National Weather Service in east Rapid City, SD (91 MPH at 4:56 AM MST on December 18) and the Rapid City Regional Airport (94 MPH at 5:13 AM MST on December 18).||Rapidly deepening low-pressure system over Saskatchewan on December 17 tracked east-southeastward into northern Minnesota on December 18. The low-pressure system dragged a warm front through the area on December 17, producing strong, gusty southwest winds up to 77 MPH in its wake. The passage of the low pushed the first cold front into the region late in the afternoon on December 17, bringing a period of strong northwest winds lasting several hours. The second, and most potent, cold front moved through the area from late evening on December 17 into the morning of December 18. Following the passage of the second cold front, historic  wind gusts were observed, persisting for 4 to 8 hours.||Some residents in the Black Hills experienced power outages that lasted a week. Further details are available in the tabs below.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet recorded wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and light snow combined to produce blizzard conditions across far northeast South Dakota. The Milbank Automated Weather Observing System (AWOS) recorded a peak wind gust of 62 mph and consistent visibilities between 1/4 and 1/2 mile. The COOP observer in Webster recorded 3.2 inches of snow, while 2.5 inches fell near LaBolt. The hazardous conditions prompted No Travel Advised notices in Roberts and Day counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Roberts County Sheriff issued No Travel Advised with the South Dakota Department of Transportation estimating visibility was 1/4 mile or less along Interstate 29. A peak wind gust of 49 mph was recorded at 642 pm by the South Dakota Department of Transportation mesonet station in Summit. The COOP observer in Sisseton recorded 2 inches of snowfall with this system as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low-pressure system and a blast of arctic air produced high winds and pockets of localized blizzard conditions across the region. The highest wind gust of 83 mph was recorded by the South Dakota Department of Transportation (SDDOT) mesonet station near Ridgeview, SD and 81 mph winds were recorded by the SDDOT mesonet station 9 miles north northwest of Vivian, SD. 26 additional weather stations would record a peak wind gust in excess of 70 mph.  High winds and light snow resulted in pockets of intermittent blizzard conditions.||High winds were responsible for blowing in the top of a grain bin in Murdo, toppling a small steel building under construction in Timber Lake, and causing a semi to jackknife near the town of Frederick in Brown County. Another semi closed portions of Highway 28 in Hamlin county after it was blown into the ditch.  Roberts, Marshall and Day counties were placed under a No Travel Advised for the duration of the system. ||Several power outages were also noted, totaling of 1647 residents, including 355 residents in Marshall and 322 residents in McPherson counties.  Day, Spink, Lyman and Stanley all suffered power infrastructure damage due to these high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds damaged a grain elevator storage bins roof. Weather stations in the surrounding area had winds top out around 70 to 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving weather system brought pockets of light freezing rain and drizzle to portions of northeast South Dakota on the morning of December 9. These conditions resulted in a light glaze of ice on area roadways, leading to hazardous travel. According to the South Dakota Highway Patrol, several slide-ins occurred near the Summit exit, and a semi-trailer jackknifed on Interstate 29 near New Effington. Emergency management and county sheriff departments issued No Travel Advised for Marshall and Roberts counties. Rainfall totals of 0.01 inches were recorded at the National Weather Service office in Aberdeen, while a trace was noted by the COOP observer in Sisseton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Marshall County Sheriff issued a No Travel Advised notice due to a light glaze of ice on roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low-pressure system zipped across northeast South Dakota, resulting in powerful northwest winds. Widespread wind gusts in excess of 58 mph were reported, with 55 total reports of high winds associated with this system. The highest recorded gust reached 77 mph at the South Dakota Department of Transportation (SDDOT) weather station in Clark. The Aberdeen Regional Airport ASOS recorded a peak gust of 74 mph. The winds caused significant damage in Aberdeen, including blown-over trees, fence damage, and shattered windows at a downtown business. Power outages affected 4,267 residents across the region, including 1,791 in Brown County and 1,192 in Spink County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic, record-breaking wind storm wreaked havoc across northeastern Wyoming and western South Dakota on December 17-18, 2025. Wind gusts reached 101 MPH, resulting in significant damage to trees, power lines, and some structures. A couple of fires were also propelled by the high winds. All-time high wind gusts were officially recorded at the National Weather Service in east Rapid City, SD (91 MPH at 4:56 AM MST on December 18) and the Rapid City Regional Airport (94 MPH at 5:13 AM MST on December 18).||Rapidly deepening low-pressure system over Saskatchewan on December 17 tracked east-southeastward into northern Minnesota on December 18. The low-pressure system dragged a warm front through the area on December 17, producing strong, gusty southwest winds up to 77 MPH in its wake. The passage of the low pushed the first cold front into the region late in the afternoon on December 17, bringing a period of strong northwest winds lasting several hours. The second, and most potent, cold front moved through the area from late evening on December 17 into the morning of December 18. Following the passage of the second cold front, historic  wind gusts were observed, persisting for 4 to 8 hours.||Some residents in the Black Hills experienced power outages that lasted a week. Further details are available in the tabs below.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Dakota Mesonet recorded wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplifying clipper system raced from southern Alberta to Wisconsin from later on December 9 through midday on December 10. Intense synoptic winds developed on the back side of the system behind the surging cold front. Widespread northwest wind gusts reached 50 to 70 mph over several hours and resulted in considerable small limb damage and some spotty infrastructure damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Synoptic northwest winds were sustained from 40 to 44 mph with a peak gust of 59 mph at a South Dakota Mesonet site 3 miles east of Carthage.

Widespread northwest wind gusts reached 50 to 70 mph over several hours and resulted in considerable small limb damage and some spotty infrastructure damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Synoptic northwest winds were occasionally sustained from 40 to 45 mph with gusts as high as 72 mph at a South Dakota Road Weather Information System site 13 miles west of Platte and 59 mph 8 miles east of Wagner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system brought a band of light to moderate snowfall across southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the early morning through midday hours of December 13. With northerly winds as much as 15 to 30 mph, a strong arctic high-pressure area plunged through the Northern Plains through December 14 with dangerously cold conditions and wind chills as low as -20 degrees F to -35 degrees F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 4.0 inches 9 miles north of Gregory and 2.0 inches 9 miles south of Dallas and at Gregory.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-moving low pressure system dropped southeastward across the Northern Plains on December 6. Light to moderate snow resulted in widespread accumulations of 2 to 6 inches. Snowfall rates ranged from 1 to 2 inches per hour within a narrow band across far southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa where snowfall totaled 6 to 10 inches. Rapid snow accumulation resulted in slick and snow covered roads, making regional travel difficult. Some patchy freezing drizzle fell as well after the bulk of the snow moved out of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 7.1 inches at Hartford and 5 to 7 inches across Sioux Falls. The official snowfall total at Sioux Falls Regional Airport was 4.9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic, record-breaking wind storm wreaked havoc across northeastern Wyoming and western South Dakota on December 17-18, 2025. Wind gusts reached 101 MPH, resulting in significant damage to trees, power lines, and some structures. A couple of fires were also propelled by the high winds. All-time high wind gusts were officially recorded at the National Weather Service in east Rapid City, SD (91 MPH at 4:56 AM MST on December 18) and the Rapid City Regional Airport (94 MPH at 5:13 AM MST on December 18).||Rapidly deepening low-pressure system over Saskatchewan on December 17 tracked east-southeastward into northern Minnesota on December 18. The low-pressure system dragged a warm front through the area on December 17, producing strong, gusty southwest winds up to 77 MPH in its wake. The passage of the low pushed the first cold front into the region late in the afternoon on December 17, bringing a period of strong northwest winds lasting several hours. The second, and most potent, cold front moved through the area from late evening on December 17 into the morning of December 18. Following the passage of the second cold front, historic  wind gusts were observed, persisting for 4 to 8 hours.||Some residents in the Black Hills experienced power outages that lasted a week. Further details are available in the tabs below.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS recorded wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure moved across North Dakota and northern Minnesota early on December 18th. A very strong cold front plowed southward behind the system and was accompanied by a minimal amount of snowfall over several hours during the later morning and afternoon hours. While most snowfall was limited to a half inch to inch at most, increasing collocation of the snow and strong wind east of Interstate 29 resulted in areas of blizzard or near blizzard conditions across parts of southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa. Several injury accidents were reported across northwest Iowa with the Department of Transportation advising no travel for several hours in the afternoon. The high winds also produced some spotty power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds were sustained between 40 and 45 mph at times, with gusts as high as 66 mph 1 mile east of Lane and 62 mph 2 miles east-southeast of Wessington Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent winter storm moved from Kansas northeastward to the Great Lakes on December 28 into the morning hours of December 29.  The system brought a leading and more prominent band of snowfall during the morning and midday on December 28, followed by a break in precipitation.  As the system deepened during the evening, the band of snowfall wrapped back southward through far eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa.  When the current and previous snow combined with winds gusting from 40 to 60 mph, widespread blizzard conditions developed from southwest Minnesota into northwest Iowa.  State Departments of Transportation advised no travel across southern Minnesota and portions of Iowa from late morning on the 28th through mid-morning of the 29th.  Numerous roadways were closed due to numerous crashes.  Total snowfall reached as high as 2 to 5 inches in much of southwest Minnesota into northwest Iowa, with 1 to 2 inches along the Missouri River and Interstate 29 corridors and lesser amounts toward the mid James River valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Short duration near-blizzard conditions occurred, divided by a longer improvement in conditions as falling snow ended for a time during the afternoon and early evening. Gusty northwest winds from 35 to 50 mph were concurrent with light snowfall, which totaled 1.5 to 2.5 inches around the Sioux Falls metro area, and resulted in brief visibility of around one-half mile that disrupted travel and caused several accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure moved across North Dakota and northern Minnesota early on December 18th. A very strong cold front plowed southward behind the system and was accompanied by a minimal amount of snowfall over several hours during the later morning and afternoon hours. While most snowfall was limited to a half inch to inch at most, increasing collocation of the snow and strong wind east of Interstate 29 resulted in areas of blizzard or near blizzard conditions across parts of southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa. Several injury accidents were reported across northwest Iowa with the Department of Transportation advising no travel for several hours in the afternoon. The high winds also produced some spotty power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds were sustained between 40 and 45 mph at times, with gusts as high as 60 mph 1 mile southeast of Madison, and 59 mph at Madison and a South Dakota Mesonet site 6 miles south of Madison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent winter storm moved from Kansas northeastward to the Great Lakes on December 28 into the morning hours of December 29.  The system brought a leading and more prominent band of snowfall during the morning and midday on December 28, followed by a break in precipitation.  As the system deepened during the evening, the band of snowfall wrapped back southward through far eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa.  When the current and previous snow combined with winds gusting from 40 to 60 mph, widespread blizzard conditions developed from southwest Minnesota into northwest Iowa.  State Departments of Transportation advised no travel across southern Minnesota and portions of Iowa from late morning on the 28th through mid-morning of the 29th.  Numerous roadways were closed due to numerous crashes.  Total snowfall reached as high as 2 to 5 inches in much of southwest Minnesota into northwest Iowa, with 1 to 2 inches along the Missouri River and Interstate 29 corridors and lesser amounts toward the mid James River valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Short duration near-blizzard conditions occurred, divided by a longer improvement in conditions as falling snow ended for a time during the afternoon and early evening. Gusty northwest winds from 30 to 45 mph were concurrent with light snowfall, which totaled 1.0 inch at De Smet, and resulted in brief visibility of one-quarter to one-half mile that disrupted travel.

All-time high wind gusts were officially recorded at the National Weather Service in east Rapid City, SD (91 MPH at 4:56 AM MST on December 18) and the Rapid City Regional Airport (94 MPH at 5:13 AM MST on December 18).||Rapidly deepening low-pressure system over Saskatchewan on December 17 tracked east-southeastward into northern Minnesota on December 18. The low-pressure system dragged a warm front through the area on December 17, producing strong, gusty southwest winds up to 77 MPH in its wake. The passage of the low pushed the first cold front into the region late in the afternoon on December 17, bringing a period of strong northwest winds lasting several hours. The second, and most potent, cold front moved through the area from late evening on December 17 into the morning of December 18. Following the passage of the second cold front, historic  wind gusts were observed, persisting for 4 to 8 hours.||Some residents in the Black Hills experienced power outages that lasted a week. Further details are available in the tabs below.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Dakota mesonet site t 1 SSE Kyle recorded wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure moved across North Dakota and northern Minnesota early on December 18th. A very strong cold front plowed southward behind the system and was accompanied by a minimal amount of snowfall over several hours during the later morning and afternoon hours. While most snowfall was limited to a half inch to inch at most, increasing collocation of the snow and strong wind east of Interstate 29 resulted in areas of blizzard or near blizzard conditions across parts of southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa. Several injury accidents were reported across northwest Iowa with the Department of Transportation advising no travel for several hours in the afternoon. The high winds also produced some spotty power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds were sustained between 40 and 46 mph at times, with gusts as high as 62 mph 4 miles south of Arlington and 58 mph at a South Dakota Mesonet site 7 miles north of De Smet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent winter storm moved from Kansas northeastward to the Great Lakes on December 28 into the morning hours of December 29.  The system brought a leading and more prominent band of snowfall during the morning and midday on December 28, followed by a break in precipitation.  As the system deepened during the evening, the band of snowfall wrapped back southward through far eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa.  When the current and previous snow combined with winds gusting from 40 to 60 mph, widespread blizzard conditions developed from southwest Minnesota into northwest Iowa.  State Departments of Transportation advised no travel across southern Minnesota and portions of Iowa from late morning on the 28th through mid-morning of the 29th.  Numerous roadways were closed due to numerous crashes.  Total snowfall reached as high as 2 to 5 inches in much of southwest Minnesota into northwest Iowa, with 1 to 2 inches along the Missouri River and Interstate 29 corridors and lesser amounts toward the mid James River valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained northwest winds reached 40 to 44 mph with a peak gust of 61 mph 13 miles west of Platte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-moving low pressure system dropped southeastward across the Northern Plains on December 6. Light to moderate snow resulted in widespread accumulations of 2 to 6 inches. Snowfall rates ranged from 1 to 2 inches per hour within a narrow band across far southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa where snowfall totaled 6 to 10 inches. Rapid snow accumulation resulted in slick and snow covered roads, making regional travel difficult. Some patchy freezing drizzle fell as well after the bulk of the snow moved out of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 8.0 inches at Canistota, 7.5 inches at Bridgewater, and 5.0 inches at Montrose and 5 miles northeast of Salem.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure developed across northern South Dakota and pushed eastward into Minnesota on the night of November 25-26. A very intense pressure gradient on the western flank of the system produced widespread winds which gusted from 50 to 75 mph, with the highest wind gusts noted through the Missouri River corridor and along the Buffalo Ridge. Light snowfall wrapped through areas of east central South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and evening, with amounts varying from a dusting along Interstate 90 to 1 to 2 inches near U.S. Highway 14. Combined with the strong winds, visibility briefly dropped to one-half mile or less in numerous locations north of Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached as high as 42 mph at a South Dakota Mesonet site near White Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of light and fluffy snow developed across north central and northeast South Dakota. Observers at the National Weather Service at the Aberdeen Regional Airport recorded a total of 8.6 inches of snow, with 8 inches measured by both the COOP in Pollock and by a resident 5 miles south of Clark. Several slide-ins were noted in the Watertown area, and a semi was tipped and rolled on Interstate 29 near Castlewood. Watertown Fire Rescue performed a vehicle extraction after a half-ton pickup rolled onto its side, leaning over a bridge with the bridge beam trapping the driver near the Estelline exit.

A strong moisture-laden Colorado Low moved across the Northern Plains November 28 and 29 resulting in widespread snowfall from 4 to 12 inches, with the greatest snowfall in portions of northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota toward the U.S. Highway 71 corridor. Many locations found a brief break in snowfall later on November 28 when some light freezing rain and freezing drizzle occurred. Overall, area roadways were snow covered and slick resulting in hazardous travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long-duration snowfall totaled 3.5 inches 11 miles north of Colman and 3.0 inches at Flandreau.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two distinct supercell clusters developed across central South Dakota during the late afternoon of July 28 within a highly unstable environment (MUCAPE up to 5000 J/kg) ahead of an eastward advancing surface front. The southern supercell cluster produced three tornadoes across Gregory County, the strongest of which was rated EF2. ||Scattered severe wind gusts were reported across southeast South Dakota as the thunderstorms moved east before the two clusters merged near and south of Sioux Falls. Aided by increasing deep layer shear from the north, a linear structure rapidly developed with a derecho racing eastward into northwest Iowa. Winds gusted from 80 to 100 mph along the derecho's path with widespread tree and structural damage across the area. In addition, three embedded short-lived tornadoes were documented within the line of thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was the third tornado documented by a storm chaser across Gregory County. This tornado touched down west of Whetstone Bay on the Missouri River, north of Whetstone Township Highway and to the west of County Road 57.  After a very short distance, the tornado broke multiple power poles along County Road 57 as it traveled south-southeast. The tornado then traveled for 5 miles across unpopulated complex terrain with no road access, although its damage path was visible on Copernicus Sentinel data 2025. Coming out of the hills, the tornado hit a single family dwelling, with the collapse of all exterior walls among other major damage. Two nearby outbuildings were also destroyed. The tornado continued to the south-southeast for approximately 1.5 miles before it flipped a camper and destroyed an abandoned trailer on the north side of Bonesteel. The tornado lifted a few hundred yards north of Eldeen Avenue, missing the city-center of Bonesteel. The tornado was rated EF2 with an estimated maximum wind gust of 135 mph. The average path width was estimated at 125 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beneath an expansive and strong, flat ridge aloft, extreme surface-based moisture returned near a diffuse warm frontal boundary on July 26 and lasted into the afternoon of July 28.  While this moisture and heat would eventually fuel the derecho during the evening hours of July 28, dangerous heat indices from 105 to 120 degrees were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerous heat index readings reached an afternoon peak on July 27 as high as 116 degrees at Madison, 112 degrees near Wentworth, 109 degrees near Ramona, and 107 degrees at South Dakota Mesonet site MDMS2 6 miles south of Madison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Locally strong winds developed on the morning of November 2nd across northeast South Dakota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Locally strong winds developed in the evening of November 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of light and fluffy snow developed across north central and northeast South Dakota. Several slide-ins were noted in the Watertown area, and a semi was tipped and rolled on Interstate 29 near Castlewood. Watertown Fire Rescue performed a vehicle extraction after a half-ton pickup rolled onto its side, leaning over a bridge with the bridge beam trapping the driver near the Estelline exit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure developed across northern South Dakota and pushed eastward into Minnesota on the night of November 25-26. A very intense pressure gradient on the western flank of the system produced widespread winds which gusted from 50 to 75 mph, with the highest wind gusts noted through the Missouri River corridor and along the Buffalo Ridge. Light snowfall wrapped through areas of east central South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and evening, with amounts varying from a dusting along Interstate 90 to 1 to 2 inches near U.S. Highway 14. Combined with the strong winds, visibility briefly dropped to one-half mile or less in numerous locations north of Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds from 40 to 55 mph along with a period of afternoon snowfall which measured up to one inch at De Smet led to visibility as low as one-half mile.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 47 mph, with gusts measured on South Dakota Road Weather Information System sites as high as 75 mph at Fort Randall Dam, 64 mph 2 miles east of Dixon, and 64 mph at Dallas. The South Dakota Mesonet site 16 miles north of Gregory measured a gust to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low-pressure system initially started with rain, followed by rapidly falling temperatures and a transition to snow. This was accompanied by strong winds across central, north central, and northeast South Dakota. The resultant icy roads and white-out conditions triggered several No Travel Advisories by Emergency Management in conjunction with the Department of Transportation, including Corson, Campbell, Dewey, Walworth, Hand, Marshall, Day, Clark, Hamlin, Grant, and Roberts counties. Conditions deteriorated significantly in Roberts County, where the Sheriff's Office responded to 139 calls for service, 65 vehicle slide-ins, 2 accidents with injuries, 7 jackknifed semi-trucks, a backup of an estimated 50+ vehicles, and a rolled snowplow. Interstate 29 was closed by mid-afternoon and remained closed until the morning of November 26th. The strong winds also resulted in power disruptions, with 1,445 customers losing power in Day County and 1,134 customers in Brown County. Winds at the South Dakota Department of Transportation Mesonet station near Frederick measured 69 mph, while the station near Miranda peaked at 68 mph. Twenty-seven additional weather stations recorded a peak wind gust at or in excess of 55 mph. As much as 8 inches of snow was recorded with this system, measured by the COOP observer 4 miles north of Victor in Roberts County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jones county sheriff reports that a camper was blown off the interstate 4 miles east of Murdo.

The first snow of the season occurred November 8 as a clipper system interacted with a polar cold-air intrusion across the Northern Plains. Light snow first developed during the early morning hours and continued into the early afternoon. A broad swath of snow from 3-5 inches fell from near Mitchell to Sioux Falls to Spencer. Relatively warm ground temperatures limited accumulations on area roadways with most major arteries clear by the afternoon. There was a report of about 6,000 customers without power in Sioux Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.0 inches at De Smet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms erupted within a very moist and unstable environment across central South Dakota during the afternoon of July 29 in response to an approaching upper level short-wave from Colorado. These thunderstorms quickly grew upscale into a line, producing 55 to 75 mph wind gusts across southeast South Dakota, resulting in isolated tree damage. In addition, prolonged heavy rain resulted in some localized flash flooding across northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts knocked down several trees and tore off part of a roof of a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture pooled in the vicinity of a nearly stationary boundary early on July 21, which slowly worked northward as a warm front over the next two days while a large upper level ridge expanded across the Northern Plains. Most areas experienced heat index values from 100 to 115 degrees, with the most consistent and highest heat index values on the afternoon of July 23 across far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa, and northeast Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerous heat index readings reached afternoon peaks as high as 115 degrees near Dakota Dunes on July 23.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms fired along a convergence zone pushing southeast across southeast South Dakota during the afternoon, with instability as high as 1500-2500 J/kg and modest effective shear of 25 to 35 knots.  While storm shear and cold pool dynamics remained balanced, isolated large hail and damaging wind gusts gradually transitioned to the latter before weakening during the mid-evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 61 mph wind gust was measured at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site near Gayville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low-pressure system initially started with rain, followed by rapidly falling temperatures and a transition to snow. This was accompanied by strong winds across central, north central, and northeast South Dakota. The resultant icy roads and white-out conditions triggered several No Travel Advisories by Emergency Management in conjunction with the Department of Transportation, including Corson, Campbell, Dewey, Walworth, Hand, Marshall, Day, Clark, Hamlin, Grant, and Roberts counties. Conditions deteriorated significantly in Roberts County, where the Sheriff's Office responded to 139 calls for service, 65 vehicle slide-ins, 2 accidents with injuries, 7 jackknifed semi-trucks, a backup of an estimated 50+ vehicles, and a rolled snowplow. Interstate 29 was closed by mid-afternoon and remained closed until the morning of November 26th. The strong winds also resulted in power disruptions, with 1,445 customers losing power in Day County and 1,134 customers in Brown County. Winds at the South Dakota Department of Transportation Mesonet station near Frederick measured 69 mph, while the station near Miranda peaked at 68 mph. Twenty-seven additional weather stations recorded a peak wind gust at or in excess of 55 mph. As much as 8 inches of snow was recorded with this system, measured by the COOP observer 4 miles north of Victor in Roberts County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility at the Britton AWOS dropped to 1/4 mile at 8:15 am and remained at or below that threshold through 4:15 pm. Winds increased notably at 12:15 pm, with a peak wind gust of 40 mph measured by the station at 1:55 pm, and gusts of 30 to 40 mph persisting through 10:00 pm. Visibility improved rapidly once snowfall intensity diminished, despite the persistent winds. The COOP observer in Britton measured 7 inches of snow. The Marshall County Emergency Manager issued a No Travel Advised statement which remained in place through the morning of Wednesday, November 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first snow of the season occurred November 8 as a clipper system interacted with a polar cold-air intrusion across the Northern Plains. Light snow first developed during the early morning hours and continued into the early afternoon. A broad swath of snow from 3-5 inches fell from near Mitchell to Sioux Falls to Spencer. Relatively warm ground temperatures limited accumulations on area roadways with most major arteries clear by the afternoon. There was a report of about 6,000 customers without power in Sioux Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 4.7 inches in Canton and 4.5 inches in Harrisburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A west-to-east oriented broken line of thunderstorms along the South Dakota-Nebraska border near sunrise on July 23 rapidly lifted north the remainder of the morning in response to a strong low-level jet. Damaging wind gusts were observed across portions of Aurora and Beadle Counties with the Huron Regional Airport (KHON) reporting a wind gust of 82 mph. Tree damage was reported across Huron and Plankinton, South Dakota with isolated structural damage in other parts of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rapidly developed across northeast South Dakota into west central Minnesota during the early evening of July 27 within a volatile environment (MUCAPE 5000-6000 J/Kg) ahead of a southward moving surface boundary. These thunderstorms expanded into a mesoscale convective system and dropped south across the region through the remainder of the evening into the early overnight hours on July 28. Most reported wind gusts ranged from 55 to 65 mph with one location reporting a gust to 70 mph. Isolated tree damage was also observed across northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS employee measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms erupted within a very moist and unstable airmass ahead of a slow moving frontal boundary during the evening of July 23. These thunderstorms bisected the city of Sioux Falls from southwest to northeast with neighborhoods southeast of I-229 reporting 2 to 5 inches of rain in a matter of 1-2 hours. This resulted in significant flash flooding across impacted portions of the city. Later in the evening, a large mesoscale convective system across Nebraska moved north and east, bringing heavy rain to portions of southeast South Dakota. Additional localized flash flooding was observed after midnight in Beresford, South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roadways and intersections were inundated by water deep enough to impede travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two distinct supercell clusters developed across central South Dakota during the late afternoon of July 28 within a highly unstable environment (MUCAPE up to 5000 J/kg) ahead of an eastward advancing surface front. The southern supercell cluster produced three tornadoes across Gregory County, the strongest of which was rated EF2. ||Scattered severe wind gusts were reported across southeast South Dakota as the thunderstorms moved east before the two clusters merged near and south of Sioux Falls. Aided by increasing deep layer shear from the north, a linear structure rapidly developed with a derecho racing eastward into northwest Iowa. Winds gusted from 80 to 100 mph along the derecho's path with widespread tree and structural damage across the area. In addition, three embedded short-lived tornadoes were documented within the line of thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused roof and wall damage to an outbuilding and destroyed two other barns, as well as broke glass in farmhouse windows. Multiple large limbs were snapped off trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure developed across northern South Dakota and pushed eastward into Minnesota on the night of November 25-26. A very intense pressure gradient on the western flank of the system produced widespread winds which gusted from 50 to 75 mph, with the highest wind gusts noted through the Missouri River corridor and along the Buffalo Ridge. Light snowfall wrapped through areas of east central South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and evening, with amounts varying from a dusting along Interstate 90 to 1 to 2 inches near U.S. Highway 14. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds periodically climbed over 40 mph and reached a peak of 44 mph at a South Dakota Road Weather Information System site at Kimball.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two distinct supercell clusters developed across central South Dakota during the late afternoon of July 28 within a highly unstable environment (MUCAPE up to 5000 J/kg) ahead of an eastward advancing surface front. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure developed across northern South Dakota and pushed eastward into Minnesota on the night of November 25-26. A very intense pressure gradient on the western flank of the system produced widespread winds which gusted from 50 to 75 mph, with the highest wind gusts noted through the Missouri River corridor and along the Buffalo Ridge. Light snowfall wrapped through areas of east central South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and evening, with amounts varying from a dusting along Interstate 90 to 1 to 2 inches near U.S. Highway 14. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds periodically reached 40 to 44 mph at a South Dakota Road Weather Information System site near Gayville with a peak gust of 59 mph. An Automated Weather Observing System site at Yankton measured sustained winds up to 44 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low-pressure system initially started with rain, followed by rapidly falling temperatures and a transition to snow. This was accompanied by strong winds across central, north central, and northeast South Dakota. The resultant icy roads and white-out conditions triggered several No Travel Advisories by Emergency Management in conjunction with the Department of Transportation, including Corson, Campbell, Dewey, Walworth, Hand, Marshall, Day, Clark, Hamlin, Grant, and Roberts counties. Conditions deteriorated significantly in Roberts County, where the Sheriff's Office responded to 139 calls for service, 65 vehicle slide-ins, 2 accidents with injuries, 7 jackknifed semi-trucks, a backup of an estimated 50+ vehicles, and a rolled snowplow. Interstate 29 was closed by mid-afternoon and remained closed until the morning of November 26th. The strong winds also resulted in power disruptions, with 1,445 customers losing power in Day County and 1,134 customers in Brown County. Winds at the South Dakota Department of Transportation Mesonet station near Frederick measured 69 mph, while the station near Miranda peaked at 68 mph. Twenty-seven additional weather stations recorded a peak wind gust at or in excess of 55 mph. As much as 8 inches of snow was recorded with this system, measured by the COOP observer 4 miles north of Victor in Roberts County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer in Faulkton reported blizzard conditions, which were confirmed by a report from the Cresbard area. The South Dakota Department of Transportation Mesonet station recorded a peak wind gust of 64 mph. The CoCoRaHS observer 4 miles west of Chelsea reported 3 inches of total snowfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser documented the initial touchdown of a tornado a little over a mile to the northwest of Dixon, SD just north of 276th Street in an open field. The tornado traveled to the east-southeast for around 2 miles before it impacted the north side of a farmstead where it damaged the shelter belt and destroyed an older outbuilding. As it continued along the path, it crossed near the intersection of SD 47 and SD 44 where it downed power poles and twisted a couple of SDDOT truck shelters. The tornado then traveled over mainly open fields for another 2 to 3 miles before taking a slight turn more to the southeast. After around 5 more miles on a southeastward path, the tornado lifted in an area of more uneven terrain. The tornado was rated EF1 with an estimated maximum wind gust of 110 mph. The average path width was around 100 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong moisture-laden Colorado Low moved across the Northern Plains November 28 and 29 resulting in widespread snowfall from 4 to 12 inches, with the greatest snowfall in portions of northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota toward the U.S. Highway 71 corridor. Many locations found a brief break in snowfall later on November 28 when some light freezing rain and freezing drizzle occurred. Overall, area roadways were snow covered and slick resulting in hazardous travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long-duration snowfall totaled 5.0 inches at Menno and 3.3 inches 11 miles south of Emery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two distinct supercell clusters developed across central South Dakota during the late afternoon of July 28 within a highly unstable environment (MUCAPE up to 5000 J/kg) ahead of an eastward advancing surface front. The southern supercell cluster produced three tornadoes across Gregory County, the strongest of which was rated EF2. ||Scattered severe wind gusts were reported across southeast South Dakota as the thunderstorms moved east before the two clusters merged near and south of Sioux Falls. Aided by increasing deep layer shear from the north, a linear structure rapidly developed with a derecho racing eastward into northwest Iowa. Winds gusted from 80 to 100 mph along the derecho's path with widespread tree and structural damage across the area. In addition, three embedded short-lived tornadoes were documented within the line of thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser documented the initial touchdown of a tornado less than one mile northwest of Lucas, SD a bit southeast of the intersection of 283rd Street and 347th Avenue. The tornado then tracked southeast for around one mile, narrowly missing the town of Lucas but uprooting three large pine trees at 284th Street and 348th Avenue. After crossing the intersection, the tornado shredded the tree line just to the east of a hunting lodge. The tornado traveled across an open section before turning more southerly and again impacting a tree line at the mid-section line. The tornado crossed 349th Avenue and left a track in some mature corn before lifting just east of 349th Avenue. The tornado was rated EF0 with an estimated maximum wind gust of 85 mph. The average path width was 25 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beneath an expansive and strong, flat ridge aloft, extreme surface-based moisture returned near a diffuse warm frontal boundary on July 26 and lasted into the afternoon of July 28.  While this moisture and heat would eventually fuel the derecho during the evening hours of July 28, dangerous heat indices from 105 to 120 degrees were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerous heat index readings reached an afternoon peak as high as 117 degrees near Platte, 114 degrees 13 miles west of Platte, 111 degree near Geddes, and 110 degrees near Wagner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed late afternoon along a slow-moving frontal boundary. Even with fairly weak deep-layer shear, strong instability resulted in large hail. Drier air and advancing cold pools later in development produced spotty damaging winds. High moisture content and slowly progressing storms also produced some locally heavy rainfall with rates briefly from 3 to 5 inches per hour.||A particularly intense and long-lived supercell developed near Vermillion and tracked southeast, generating hail up to golf ball size and a swath of damaging winds along Burbank Road up to 85 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees were blown down across Burbank Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a nearly stationary pre-frontal boundary during the early evening across south central South Dakota. As cold pool development forced storms eastward off the initiating boundary into an environment with appreciable DCAPE and deep-layer shear of 40 to 50 knots, thunderstorms organized into a small bowing segment, which plowed east and southeast with up to nearly 90 mph winds. After several hours, storms moved into a less favorable environment with both lesser shear and instability toward the James River Valley and weakened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper-level pattern experienced amplification of an eastern U.S. ridge and western U.S. trough, which locked a low-level frontal boundary in place from western Nebraska through central South Dakota into the Great Lakes. This allowed for heat to spread northward as very high dew point readings pooled across the area with the recent wet conditions. This multi-day dangerous heat event featured daytime heat index readings from 100 to 120 degrees, with nighttime low temperatures remaining from the mid 70s to lower 80s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daily afternoon and evening heat indices ranged from 105 to 111 degrees with a maximum heat index of 111 degrees at the Road Weather Information System site near Cavour and 108 degrees near Wolsey on June 21, and 106 degrees at the Automated Surface Observing System site at Huron on June 21.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated storms re-intensified in the early morning hours on the nose of the low-level jet. With effective shear around 30 knots and around 1000 J/kg MUCAPE, storms were able to organize into a short bowing segment, which produced a short path of damaging wind gusts across east central South Dakota with speeds measured up to around 75 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds were measured by South Dakota Mesonet station DTMS2 De Smet 7N.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated storms re-intensified in the early morning hours on the nose of the low-level jet. With effective shear around 30 knots and around 1000 J/kg MUCAPE, storms were able to organize into a short bowing segment, which produced a short path of damaging wind gusts across east central South Dakota with speeds measured up to around 75 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were measured by a personal weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge rider pattern continued across the region, with a volatile but slightly capped environment in place heading into the evening of June 28.  Remnant boundaries from convection the prior morning provided focus for the new storm development, which had up to 3000 J/kg MUCAPE and around 30 knots of effective shear.  Storms remained fairly restrained in the Missouri River Valley fighting the warmest air aloft and weakest shear profiles. Storms coming from northeast South Dakota became the most organized, with a small bowing segment producing damaging winds up to around 65 mph.  Other late night storms strengthened moving from far southeast South Dakota across northwest Iowa with wind gusts up to 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong storms spread between north central and northeast South Dakota producing high winds and tornadoes. A peak gust of 70 mph was measured at the Mobridge Regional Airport and a 68 mph gust was measured on a personal weather station 3 miles north-northwest of Lake Cochrane. Two tornadoes developed; one tracked approximately 4 miles causing damage to farmsteads and vehicles, while the second was a brief touchdown with no observed damage.

Several strong storms spread between north central and northeast South Dakota producing high winds and tornadoes. A peak gust of 70 mph was measured at the Mobridge Regional Airport and a 68 mph gust was measured on a personal weather station 3 miles north-northwest of Lake Cochrane. Two tornadoes developed; one tracked approximately 4 miles causing damage to farmsteads and vehicles, while the second was a brief touchdown with no observed damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado tracked north-northwest over mainly open country near the city of Mobridge. This tornado initially hit a farmstead where damage was observed to a grain silo. It then tipped two semi-trailers on SD Highway 1804. The tornado destroyed an outbuilding at another farmstead located a mile south of US Highway 12. As the tornado crossed US Highway 12, it overturned a camper being pulled by a vehicle and bent a power pole. After crossing US Highway 12, the tornado tracked just west of 290th Avenue, taking down a series of six power poles that were either completely broken or severely bent before the tornado dissipated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper-level pattern experienced amplification of an eastern U.S. ridge and western U.S. trough, which locked a low-level frontal boundary in place from western Nebraska through central South Dakota into the Great Lakes. This allowed for heat to spread northward as very high dew point readings pooled across the area with the recent wet conditions. This multi-day dangerous heat event featured daytime heat index readings from 100 to 120 degrees, with nighttime low temperatures remaining from the mid 70s to lower 80s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daily afternoon and evening heat indices ranged from 105 to 108 degrees with a maximum heat index of 108 degrees at the Road Weather Information System site near Lane on June 20 and the Automated Surface Observing System site at Wessington Springs on June 21.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy rainfall occurred across a broad portion of Stanley and Dewey counties. The COOP observer 3 miles west southwest of Wendt would record a 2 day total of 4.94 while the CoCoRaHS observer in Fort Pierre received 4.06 over 3 days. This area had also received about an inch of rainfall on or around the 15th. Rainfall and saturated soils resulted in flooding of several streams and creeks, including the Bad River between Wendt and Fort Pierre, and Virgin Creek near Promise. Shelters were opened for homeowners impacted by rises in the Bad River in Fort Pierre, with portions of Bad River road seeing damage due to washouts. A stretch of BIA 8 east of Eagle Butte was also partly washed out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gauge on the Little Moreau at Promise went above flood stage on the afternoon of September 18th, topping out at 23.03 feet (2.03 feet above flood stage) during the early morning hours of September 19th. The river fell below flood stage later that afternoon. Virgin Creek, which flows into the Little Moreau just before they enter the Missouri River, was flowing across Promise Road at a depth of over one foot.
